- How Much My Indie Game Made with 1 Million Android Downloads: Kind: captions Language: en Have you ever wondered how much money can a mobile game make with 1 million downloads on iOS or Android? I did because recently one of my games reached this milestone of 1 million downloads. Check this out. If you think tons of downloads equals tons of money, well, let me stop you right there. Some games with million downloads get hundred thousand of dollars, while others just a few grand. In my case, is more or less average. My game was well, it's pretty old. I made it in 2020 and overall it kind of sucks. Bad level design, boring mechanics, awful on boarding. But despite all of that, it actually made a pretty solid amount of money, especially for being my second ever project. So, what was the smart move I did with my stupid game? Porting it to as many platforms as I could. And honestly, I think that's something that every indie dev should do to be able to make living out of making games. So, in this video, I'll break down how much it made just on Android from more than 1 million downloads and then how much it earned in total across all other platform I ported to. And those are the major ones like Android, iOS, Xbox, Switch, Steam, and even HTML 5. But before that, I'll quickly introduce you the game I'm talking about. So the game is called Enderance and it's a pixel art sci-fi topown shooter and I released it in June 2020 on Android and iOS. So I made this game within 4 months almost entirely solo. I was getting help from a pixel artist and very talented guy who handled the music and sound effects. The rest was on me. Story, design, code, marketing. By the way, about marketing this game and getting featured on iOS and Android, I talk in my video right here. And you may wonder what were the game metrics. They were totally bad. They are still totally bad. But at the time, I didn't know that. So, day one retention was around 15% and that means that 85% of users were dropping the game just after installing it. And average play time was around 19 minutes. And the game is 4 hours long. And the game had very stupid design problems like no mid-level saves, long and punishing levels. And if you die, you have to start from scratch from the very very beginning of the level. Really hardcore. The repetitive game play, lame boss fights, and auto aim mechanic probably made things even worse. But the story and overall ambience, they were not bad. And a lot of players actually stuck around just to see how it ended. and that meant a lot. So, if you got bored with that introduction, let's get back to the main question. How much money did it with more than 1 million downloads on Android? Here's the breakdown. So, from ads, by the way, I'm using Yoda Mass Mediation for ads and it made $26,626. So, I have two versions of the game on Google Play, the premium one and the free one. So, the free one comes with ads. And from the free one, I got $12,000. And from the paid one, I got $45,000. In the very beginning, when I just released the game, I used ADM Mob Mediation, and it brought me almost $2,000. So, the total Android revenue with more than 1 million downloads brought me $85,447. and that with with some marketing expenses but mostly organic downloads and even now in 2025 it still brings me few hundred bucks every month and that's more than some of my newer prototypes are making. So not bad at all. Now let's look at all platforms combined. So downloads from Android are more than 1 million and from iOS 229,000. So, the revenue from Android, as I said before, it's $85,000. iOS one brought me from ads $7,367 and from the inner purchases $9,390. Xbox over these years brought me more or less $13,000. Nintendo Switch tiny bit more than $3,000. Steam and HTML 5 were the weakest ones. Steam brought $625. and HTML 5 brought $150. So the total lifetime revenue is $119,179. Sadly, all that was spread within these years. And here's the thing, the game shouldn't be still making any money, but it does. And why? Because I made it with heart and I put it everywhere. When I look back, yeah, there are million things I would change, but when I boot it up, it still makes me proud. It may be rough around the edges, but it's real. I didn't just prototype some gameplay loop and call it a day. I built a full experience as messy as it was. And all this before AI tools were a thing. There were moments I spent 3 4 days stuck on a bug that would take 30 seconds to solve with Chad GPT. Today, I Googled everything. I used Stack Overflow, Unity Forums. By the way, anyone still using those, but I pushed through. I released it and it mattered more than I expected. And then came the smartest decision I ever made. Ported to everything. Straight after releasing it, I started getting offers from publishers and I signed them. Steam didn't make much sadly and left me disappointed. So, I kind of gave up on Steam. But maybe one day I'll get back to it. But together, every platform earned just enough. And those tiny streams added up. Sometimes surviving as an indie game deaf isn't about making one hit. It's about making one decent thing and making it available everywhere. So no, endurance didn't change the world, but it got featured on App Store and Google Play. Though it didn't make me rich, but it paid some bills and it founded my future projects. and it gave me something no spreadsheet or KPI ever could. Confidence. And I'm still proud of this game when I return and play it sometimes. If you're a solid, don't wait for perfection. Make something you care about. Release it, learn, port it. Even if this game isn't great, if it's real, people will feel that. And who knows, 5 years from now, it might be still sending you money while you're working on something new. - How I Coded ANOTHER Profitable App SOLO (step by step / from scratch / with AI): Kind: captions Language: en Another day, another startup. Recently, I moved to Malaysia and from there, I've pretty much been spending most of my time coding my latest project. And it's been super fun hacking away at something new again. And luckily, I've been documenting the whole journey from coming up with the app idea to building the app to getting that first paying customer. In this video, I'll show you every step of the process so you get an idea of what it takes to build and ship a profitable app from scratch completely solo. And feel free to stick around until the end to see how much money my app actually made. My app idea is just a simple tool that allows me to download video transcripts in bulk from YouTube channels or playlists. And you're probably asking, why do I want a tool like this? Well, it's no secret that YouTube is a gold mine of knowledge and information, but it's all trapped in video format. So, I'm building this tool for one main reason. I want a quick and easy way to extract all this gold from YouTube videos in text format so that I can feed this data directly into my own custom AI models to help me with everyday tasks like writing better YouTube scripts or creating custom AI agents to help me with my startups. Then after giving it some more thought, I know I'm not the only one who needs this tool. Pretty much anyone wanting to train their own custom AI models with YouTube content is facing the same problem. Manual transcript extraction from YouTube is painfully slow and tedious and currently there aren't many good solutions out there. So then I thought why not try building this tool myself. It solves a real problem I am facing and it could potentially be another stream of income if I execute and market it well to the other users who are facing the same problem. But before I start building, I need to do some quick market research to see what my competitors are offering. First, there's YouTube transcript.io IO, which offers bulk downloading but requires a subscription with a credit system. So, I can definitely see an opportunity for my app to compete by offering a simpler pricing model. And from looking at their landing page, they report having over 350,000 users worldwide, which is a clear sign there's already real demand for this type of product. This gives me even more confidence in my idea and extra motivation to compete with them to take a slice of the market. There are also other sites like Note GBT and Tactic, but those sites only let you download transcripts. one video at a time. So, my approach will be simple. Create a tool with a simpler pricing model and a super clean interface that makes downloading YouTube transcripts in bulk cheap, quick, and painless. After scouting out my competitors, I was ready to jump into my code editor and start building. But before I could write a single line of code, I had to plan out what the first version of my app would be like. Otherwise, I'll either end up building something that's not useful, or I'll constantly get distracted adding shiny new features that don't actually solve the root problem. I prefer using the SLC framework to plan the early versions of my app. I'm not a fan of shipping MVPs that are buggy at launch and don't do the key one to two features they promise just for the sake of speed or shipping fast. The SLC framework stands for simple, lovable, and complete. And I think it's a much better approach than just building something that's minimum and viable. For my app to be simple, it needs to have one straightforward path to solving the root problem, and that's extracting YouTube transcripts in bulk. This means users should be able to complete the task in as few steps or clicks as possible. Nobody likes using a halfbaked product for version one. This means users should be able to download thousands of transcripts quickly. My app won't be feature- packed at launch, but it will be complete. This means users should be able to sign up, make a purchase, and immediately download transcripts. This is the leanest possible version of my app and the goal is to provide value from day one and then iterate the product based on real feedback from users. Like I've said before, I'm aiming to ship a version one of something simple instead of a version 0.1 of something complex. Now that I have a basic plan in place, it's time to commit and secure a domain for the app. I decided to go with YouTube transcripts.io as it's short, descriptive, SEOfriendly, and doesn't break the bank. Now that the domain was purchased, it was time to start building. For this project, I'm using the standard web text stack, React and Next.js for my front end and API, Superbase for my database and authentication, and Stripe to handle payments. Normally, setting up my projects would take me a few hours to get everything connected and working. But this time, I am using Tempo to help me with the setup process, and I was able to get my OR, database, and payments all working in under 10 minutes. All I had to do was click through their guided setup, paste in a few API keys and within minutes I had a fully functioning project with all the annoying stuff correctly configured. Then I gave a simple prompt to Tempo describing what my app does. And then it just starts generating the project files, documenting project requirements, and building out the UI. And honestly, the first version of the landing page tempo generated was pretty impressive. It wasn't perfect, but it was a solid starting point with a clean layout and some decent copy. From there, I gave the AI a few more prompts to refine some sections, like updating the feature section to better highlight what the app actually does, adding a use cases section and an FAQ section to answer common questions or objections up front. Before we move on, let me give you a quick tour of the Tempo UI. The interface is split into three tabs: product, design, and code. In the product tab, you can write out your product requirements, features, and even create full user flow diagrams of your app. The most useful thing about this is that all this information gets fed directly to the AI as context, which reduces hallucinations massively. In the design tab, you can build out your UI just by clicking and dragging elements pretty much just like any other design tool like Figma or Web Flow, but it generates actual React code behind the scenes. They also support importing your custom Figma designs, so you can use them as a starting point and ask the AI to generate React code for you based on the designs. Finally, the code tab is where you can open and edit any file in your project just like you would in VS Code or cursor. It also integrates nicely with GitHub, so you can push your code directly to a repo and keep everything version controlled. With the initial setup all done, I push the code to GitHub and fired up Cursor so that I can start building out the core features of my app. So, enjoy this time-lapse of me hacking away over the next couple days whilst I get this app ready for launch. [Music] Oh, hey. Heat. Heat. After days of non-stop coding and testing, I think the app is finally ready. It's time to launch and get some users. My plan for the initial launch is pretty basic. I'll post on all of the typical launch platforms like Product Hunt, Hacker News, and Twitter to try my luck going viral, but I'm not banking on this too much. My tool is quite niche and solves a very specific problem. So there's really no point trying to appeal to a wider audience at this stage. And hot take, I don't think Product Hunt is actually a good platform to launch for most indie hackers. Unless you're making a product specifically targeted to other founders because the truth is regular customers aren't going to Product Hunt to find tools to solve their problems. It's usually just a bunch of other founders looking for a place to share their products. But you do get a nice backlink for SEO and a bump in traffic if you can rank highly. So, it's at least worth trying. That's why I'll be spending most of my time going directly to my ideal customers and building relationships with them first. This means finding niche subreddits and forums and sharing my launch there instead. So, how did the launch go and how much money has my app actually made? Well, I can tell you my app didn't blow up overnight. And I'm not going to lie, it was discouraging at first seeing my launch get less traction than I hoped. It's disappointing, but this won't be one of those videos where I launch my product and it magically goes viral. And honestly, I think sharing realistic launches like these can be more valuable than the overnight success stories because whilst they can be inspiring, they can also create unrealistic expectations and discourage you to keep going if you don't see immediate results. So, even though the launch wasn't what I hoped for, I'm not going to let it distract me from improving and growing my product until I can get it to a point where I'm satisfied and feel like there's nothing more I can do. Since the launch, I've managed to get 13 user signups with six of them converting to actual paying customers. And so far, my app has earned over £200 since the launch. It's not some life-changing money, but at least it's proof that people are willing to pay for my product, even at its earliest stage. So, what's next? I have a bunch of ideas and improvements I want to make to this app already. And of course, I'll be documenting everything on this channel. So, if you want to see how I grow this app from these humble beginnings into something bigger, feel free to subscribe and I'll see you in the next

- How to Make a Living from Mobile Games in 2025 (Realistic Solo Dev Strategies): Kind: captions Language: en Is it hard to make mobile games today? The simple answer is no. Is it hard to make a living out of it? Yes. But wait, there is good news as well. So sit tight. With the development of AI, you now have plenty of tools helping you speed up the process. You can create code. You can ask questions and get pretty good answers. You can create art for your games using AI, and you can even create 3D models with it. You can animate those 3D models using free tools like Mixama. And if you're stuck with ideas, you can ask AI to analyze the market and it will give you a direction. So, it's never been easier to make a mobile game than it is today. But why is it still hard to make a living out of it? Well, look at this. So many games are released every day. And if you check the top charts, most of them are coming from companies with huge budgets for ads. So, can you compete with them once you publish your game? Well, yes, if you also have a huge budget for ads. And what if you don't? What if you're poor as [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] How do you make a living out of making games then? Well, there are other ways. Way number one, make a game, publish it. Google Play or App Store will give you some initial traction, some initial downloads. Or you can boost it a bit with $100 $200 running user acquisition campaigns. If your game gets good reviews, good in-game metrics, low crash rates, and you have solid apps for optimization, the algorithm may push your game more. That means you'll start getting organic installs for a decent period of time. Check this out. I have a few games that I didn't spend money to promote, but Google Play and App Store are still showing them organically to users. So, I'm getting a steady stream of installs. So, yeah, I'd say it's an old school method, but with this approach, pretty unlikely you ever get close to the top charts. So, what's the upside? You start getting some income already. A few bucks per day, still good though. But what's the best part? Since the algorithm is showing your game to people and if the quality is decent and you'll start getting noticed by publishers, you'll start getting offers now. Choose wisely and take it. A good publisher will help your game a lot. It will handle marketing, potentially fund future updates, offer revenue share deals, or even invite you to build your next game with them. In any case, this path can help you actually make a living from games. Sure, you might lose a bit of freedom, but in return, you get resources and support. There's also another way you can make living out of making games without publishers. So, what is it? You make a game, release it, get some decent reviews, apps optimization, use AI tools to publish and test. Now, your game earns $100 to $100 per month from purely organic installs. You've published it to Google Play, App Store, HTML 5 portals for extra revenue. Now what? Make another game. Another small one. Something that takes one two months to build. Do the same. Google Play, App Store, HTML 5. Now you're earning 200, 400 per months. Then make another game. What I'm saying is build up your income with bunch of small games. It might be slow, but it gives you more chances to survive than betting everything on one hit game that will change your life. Because let's be honest, those chances are super low. So, in a year, you're probably going to have six to eight games released, and together they'll bring a decent monthly income. The hard part, you'll have to support those games minimally, just enough so they don't instantly die without updates. And by the way, one of them may take off and bring you a solid amount of income. And just imagine how many new mechanics, workflows, and tricks you'll learn within that year. That knowledge is priceless. But let's be real, during that year, you'll still need to eat and pay your bills. So, my advice, if you have a job, don't quit it until you're confident. Find someone to help you speed up the process. Share the work. Making games with other people is so much more fun than grinding solo. Way number three, there's also another way you can make games professionally as a freelancer. I know people doing this and making living out of it. You can look for jobs in gamedev communities. Browse gigs on app work or freelancer.com. Reach out to YouTubers making games asking if they need help. Do paid Unity prototyping art, ASO, even AI based optimization. If you already built and published your own games, you already have a portfolio. So, you're not just another random freelancer. You're someone who actually shipped products. That's a big plus. This lets you stay in gamedev, make money now and keep building your your own games in parallel. Way number four, you can also build games and sell them. There are platforms like FIPA where you can sell your game outright source code, assets, everything. Or you can pitch your game to publisher or platforms like crazy games. If they like your game, they might buy it or license it for the period paying you up front or giving your revenue share. This works especially well if your game is in HTML 5 format or you have a working mobile prototype with a solid design and monetization. This method isn't consistent, but if you're fast and can produce good quality prototypes, it can bring a real income. So, is it possible to make a living out of making games nowadays? Definitely, yes. Is it harder than before? Yes, absolutely. I've been making games since 2017. And since 2022, I've been doing it full-time. Am I rich? No. Was it easy to start doing it full-time? Honestly, no. But is it fun? Oh, yeah. And have I learned a lot since then? Definitely. And let me know in the comments if you know any other ways to make living out of making games. Do you agree or disagree with the ones I've shared? And drop your experience below. And of course, you know what to do with the buttons under the video. Like, comment, subscribe, or push them gently and see you in the next one. [Music]

Archives produced by older Xcode versions will be rejected at the gate. >> Check your runner configuration immediately. Ensure Xcode select points to version 26 and verify your fastlane gym flags. Do a smoke test with a TestFlight build today. Don't wait until April 29th to discover a broken pipeline in your production branch. Story two, >> Xcode 26.4.1. This point release is more important than it looks, especially for async code. It addresses a MetricKit crash on older OS versions and a critical memory safety bug in the Swift concurrency runtime. The bug involves stack allocation in async functions. It causes freed pointer crashes, >> particularly in Swift async let finish. Why now? The optimizers in Swift 6.2 and 6.3 became much more efficient, which unfortunately exposed these pre-existing bugs. MetricKit apps built with 26.4 would crash on devices running anything below iOS 26.4 due to missing symbols. The fix is [music] simple. Rebuild with Xcode 26.4.1. This is now available on all major CI platforms, including [music] CircleCI. Story three, Swift 6.3.1. Released on April 17th, this patch is bundled inside the latest Xcode release. Beyond the async fix, it resolves a Windows LLDB library path issue and a narrow but annoying bug in Swift testing. If you had a domain model named comment, the expect macro would fail to build. 6.3.1 fixes this conflict. For cross-platform teams, the LLDB fix on Windows is huge. It restores debugger functionality on no asserts toolchains. Story four, Xcode 26.5 beta. This update introduces the biggest change to StoreKit since the original StoreKit 2 release. The new pricing terms model allows you to handle 12-month commitment billing plans directly through the SDK. This architecture eliminates the need for hardcoded pricing strings. You can pull commitment metadata live from Apple's servers. Watch out for a known issue in the beta, SKTestSession may ignore your configuration. There is a documented workaround. Now, let's talk about the iOS job market. The numbers for April 2026 tell a very specific story about our industry. Number one, specialist job postings are down 60%. This [music] isn't a dip, it's a long-term shift in hiring. Number two, productivity tools like SwiftUI means smaller teams can now achieve the same throughput as massive UIKit teams. >> The result? Junior roles are compressing, but the demand for senior engineers who own the entire pipeline is surging. In 2026, standard [music] senior software engineer salaries in the US are reaching around $185,000, while US-based senior contractors can command rates of up to $180 per hour. The floor of this market is crowded. The ceiling is still wide open. >> In 2026, your best career hedge is investing in deep architecture. Define clear layers, >> design for modularity, and build CI/CD from day one. To recap, update your CI to Xcode 26, rebuild for memory safety, and start testing those new subscription APIs. - How to Monetize an App [In 2023]: Kind: captions Language: en - I started Vivino 13 years ago, and always got the same question. "The app is free, how the heck do you make money?" That's what we're gonna talk about today, how to make money from apps, and there are many ways to make money, to monetize an app in 2023. This is Raw Startup. (upbeat music) I'm the founder of Vivino, the world's largest wine app, and wine community with over 60 million users all over the world. The app has always been free, and yet we're still here. How the heck is that possible? And yes, this is something I know a thing or two about. We look at the biggest five ways to make money off apps, and I will show you examples in every single category. We look at how much money there is to be made in each category, as well as the good and the bad. This list of five doesn't have all the ways you can make money off apps, but probably 90% of how money is made in apps today. With that, let's get started. Number one, sell products or services. You basically facilitate the sale of a product or a service, and this can be done in many ways with very different complexities. From a very low complexity affiliate deal to full-on e-commerce in many countries. Examples are Vivino, we have a full marketplace in the app where users can buy wine. Amazon is another great example here. This can also be done with nonphysical products, like hotels, flights, or car rentals. In that space, we have apps like booking.com and Skyscanner. Usually you get a cut, some kind of commission on everything sold through the app, that's how you make money this way. Let's have a look at how a sale can be made through an app, and this is important because the complexity changes a lot. Affiliate, basically sending buyers to somebody else that does the e-commerce, complexity, low. Marketplace, a marketplace with many vendors, one good example here is Etsy, but Amazon also has a marketplace, complexity, high. Drop shipping, you take orders, but somebody else does most of the shipping, et cetera, complexity, medium. Full e-commerce, you run a full e-commerce shop just like Amazon, complexity, high. Now, let's look of a look at the good and the bad when it comes to selling stuff in your app, and we're starting with the good. It's a feature, and doesn't really get in the way of your app. There's a big upside if customers buy a lot, and you get a cut of that. The bad, can be low margin, really depends on the category. Hard to scale, especially internationally. Adds considerable complexity depending on the model. All in all, this is a very complicated way to make money off an app, and yes, this is something I have extensive experience in. Number two, digital subscriptions, and this is a big one, have a paid digital subscription where users pay monthly or annually. This model is very popular for many reasons, we'll get to that. This model is especially good for apps that are used on a regular basis, monthly, weekly, or even daily. Apps that use this model that you probably have heard about already are Netflix, Disney+, Max, used to be HBO Max, as well as most other streaming apps. And dating is a big category here. We even did a full video on the dating app, Smitten, check that out later. There's also a lot of health and wellbeing apps that use this model. Apps like Reflectly, the journaling apps, Strava, the biking app, MyFitness Pal for calorie counting, Headspace, the meditation app, and many, many more. When it comes to how is you charge, and what you you charge, the strategies vary a little bit. A very common way of doing it is having a free app, then adding a few features, and you pay for that, that's known as freemium. Others are paid only, maybe you get a short trial, but then after that you have to pay to use the app. Prices vary depending on the service, value, and the category. We see prices all the way from a few dollars a month all the way up to 20, $30 every single month. If you have a free app, the number of users that are willing to pay can vary from say one to 10%. My impression is that most apps are between three and 5%. Okay, let's have a look at the good and the bad. The good, instantly global, high margin, recurring and predictable. The bad, not for occasional used products, some markets and people just don't like subscriptions. All in all, this is a fantastic way to monetize an app. This is a reason why it's very, very popular. Number three, advertising and sponsorships. Basically putting ads or some kind of advertising into the app, it could look something like this, or even interstitial ads, they take over the full screen and the user has to wait. They're more intrusive, but they will pay better. You will need some volume, you'll need a lot of users to make money here, or it can be very, very targeted. Then you can make a little bit of money. Showing ads to a small group of people. Say 200 people is not gonna get you very far. You will need a lot of users, but the usage is wide, it's used in all kinds of different apps. The good, pretty easy to do. There are platforms like AdMob, and it's a pretty simple way to make money. The bad, can be intrusive. Users often don't like banners, and advertising in general takes a lot of volume to get a good revenue, usually millions of users before it becomes meaningful. Number four, sell digital goods. This is sometimes referred to as in app purchases. Basically you pay something to unlock a feature or get something special inside the app. These days it's mostly used in games where you can buy things like skins, weapons, extra lives or turns, levels. These are split into two categories, consumables and non consumables. Consumables are things that you buy, and then you use them, and then you need a new one. Non-consumables, you buy once, and that's it, maybe it unlocks a feature or something like that. The cool thing about consumables is that people keep buying them. It's almost like a subscription. This can be used outside games, but you have to be a little creative. If you know any good examples, put them in the comments. The good, instantly global. There is a willingness to pay in certain categories, especially games. The bad, not recurring, unless it's consumable, then they sort of keep coming. This is a good way to monetize, especially in certain categories. Number five, sell data. When you have an app, the usage of the app usually generates some data, and there might be people willing to pay for that. With the Vivino for example, we know a lot about what people like to drink, what's trending upwards, and what's falling out of favor. This data can have a lot of value for people in the wine industry, and it is something we make money from at Vivino. All that said, please be very careful. It's okay to sell some high level data, but you can never sell user data. We would never do that. The rules are very strict, especially in Europe with GDPR, those rules are coming in the US too. I think California is leading the pack. The EU will give you a massive fine if you break those rules. Meta just got a $1.2 billion fine for selling personal data, so be very careful, or just don't do it. On top of that, apple really values privacy. They do not want you to sell any sort of user data. You are making an app, and you should really be friends with Apple. Anonymized data about users and behaviors, trends and so on is okay, but any sort of user data is a no-go. A lot of hustlers in this space, they'll try to put some SDK into your app, and sell the data, stay away from that. And the good, doesn't get in the way of the product. There is money to be made in some categories. The bad, so many things, especially on what you sell, so be careful here. All in all, this is a bit of a minefield, so if in doubt, don't do it. Here's the list of the full monetization strategies. Very often you can combine things, and then you have a really good revenue. This way you monetize different kinds of users. Some users are willing to pay for the app, others are willing to see ads. If you know other creative ways of making money on apps, please let me know in the comments. If you want more content just like this, please consider pressing Like, and subscribe, and you won't miss any future videos. Thank you very much for watching Raw Startup. Now stop watching, go build something.

And to set that up, I'll go into Lovable and type in integrate Stripe to monetize the app. Offer a free and unlimited plan model. Basic daily sleep tracking on free plan for 7 days. And after those seven days, the user needs to subscribe to the unlimited plan for $29 yearly that includes all features like daily sleep tracking, sleep schedule based on personalized schedules, analytics, and a sound library. Show the Stripe checkout for the payment process. Now, next, I'll approve the SQL commands by clicking apply changes. And then I'm going to head over to the Stripe API keys page. And from there, I'll copy the secret key and paste it into Lovable. Now, in the app header, there's a pricing section. And when I click it, I can choose between using the app for free or subscribing to the unlimited plan for $29 a year to unlock everything we talked about. And after that, I'm locked out of the app features until I subscribe. At this point, some errors do show up during the checkout process. So, I'm going to fix them step by step. This includes updating the Stripe price ID, configuring the web hook secret key, and connecting the correct endpoint URL from the Stripe web hook dashboard. I also added the updated web hook destination URL using Superbase Edge Functions. Now, once everything's wired up properly, I'll just publish the app to enable the Stripe checkout flow. Then I'll create a new account. Click subscribe to the unlimited plan. Hit subscribe now and it successfully redirects to the Stripe checkout page. There you have it, an entire baby sleep tracker built step by step, powered entirely by AI. And what used to take a whole dev team and a six-month timeline just took us a handful of prompts. multiplatform wait you said you won't talk about react native no I said maybe later and now is that later unlike on Native where you have separate code bases for each operating system with crossplatform you can have a shared codebase and deploy your app on Android on iOS and sometimes even on web and on desktop which can increase both the consistency and the efficiency of app development but how can this be how does it work well they provide an abstraction layer on top of the platform specific code so I have a unified interface to interact with each smartphone's capabilities this abstraction layer allows developers to write code in JavaScript or in Dart while still being able to access device specific features of course they are not a silver bullet solution for everything and they might come with limitations and tradeoff when it comes to Performance stability and functionalities so let's give priority to the elders and start with re native it was the first such framework that really became popular developed by Facebook in 2015 re native allows us to create mobile apps using JavaScript and the react Library it was the first solution that offered both a native F and a shared code base so it no wonder that it increased so much in popularity as a fun fact Facebook said it was created in a hecaton when they wanted to bring the ease and efficiency from on the UI side just like in react you are using jsx for creating user interfaces it's an extension for JavaScript that kind of looks like XML and then react native Bridges this gap between jsx and Native components using the so-called Bridge which means that on iOS it will render native iOS components and on Android it will render native Android components this is quite impressive and it gives you that native feel and user experience that we mentioned before as for tools the most popular IDE is Visual Studio code and you will also use the react native command line interface to build and run your projects also probably the best tool we can mention here is Expo which is actually a set of tools for react native that simplifies development even more expo comes with an additional set of JavaScript apis that cover the most common features in mobile app development like push notifications for example and you can Implement that without writing any native code code you can even build and run your apps on your phone without installing xcode or Android Studio remember what I said earlier about building iOS only on a Mac well Expo is that single other way that you can build for iOS terms of the synex here's how it looks on the UI side flatter is known for its highly customizability and for its fast rendering this is possible due to the fact that flatter doesn't rely on Native components but it has its own rendering engine called skia this means that components are rendered on a canvas and they are not translated into native iOS and Native Android components like on react native the fun and tricky part about this is that you can create a UI that looks nothing like the operating system you're building for for example you can even show the native iOS alert on Android It will bring a compounding free traffic over time. And even if one day you want to sell the tool, having SEO traffic is also very valuable. And automate as much as you can use automation tools. So like outrank, story short, etc. to automate posting, article creation, as much as you can. Finally, once you have growth and customers coming daily, I would finish the product, bring new feature, etc. >> All right. Well, that's great advice. I love your strategy. I love the business that you're building. Thank you for coming on the channel, Samuel. And I hope everyone watching this learned something and you're going to build cool stuff because of it. Thanks for coming on. >> Thanks, bud. >> I love Samuel's story because it goes to show that you don't need to invent a brand new idea. You can go find something that's already working and just make it a little bit better. With the right process and the right approach, anybody can build something successful. - EASILY Get 10,000 App Downloads in 2025 with THESE Strategy: Kind: captions Language: en App A App B They both do the same thing. If you compare the two, App A has better UI/UX, better customer support, better pricing, Better everything, basically. BUT App B has better visibility. It's on the first page of app store&nbsp; search results and App A isn’t. App A may be a better product, but App B will have more views and downloads. All things being equal, app B will&nbsp; be a more profitable business. In this video, I’ll be covering App&nbsp; Store Optimization, also known as ASO,&nbsp;&nbsp; the science behind giving&nbsp; visibility to your app and&nbsp;&nbsp; converting that visibility&nbsp; into views and downloads. Let me caveat this by saying that&nbsp; App Store Optimization is marketing,&nbsp;&nbsp; not product development. It supports a valid app. It's not&nbsp; going to save an app that doesn’t work,&nbsp;&nbsp; or a solution to a problem that doesn’t exist. Of course, marketing and product development&nbsp; should absolutely go hand in hand. I just want to be clear on where&nbsp; this adds value to your project. Why Do You Need to Learn ASO? Dear non-tech founder, Say you’ve created an MVP&nbsp; with a no code app builder. You’re gonna want to validate it with real users. You don’t want to ask your grandma. Actually, if old ladies are your&nbsp; target demographic, then by all means. Point is, you’ll need to source authentic users. Second situation, you’re&nbsp; hiring an in-house team and&nbsp;&nbsp; need to evaluate someone’s marketing knowledge. In both these cases, you’ll need to&nbsp; understand App Store Optimization. ASO is not a technical topic. You should know enough to do a decent job yourself&nbsp;&nbsp; or make sure the person you hire&nbsp; knows what they’re talking about. And that’s all I’m covering, just the&nbsp; basics of ASO for Google Play Store and&nbsp;&nbsp; Apple’s App Store to get any non-tech&nbsp; founder covered on the fundamentals. There are slight differences between Google and&nbsp; Apple, but its always two main stakeholders: The algorithm, and Users Before we start, a quick Public Service&nbsp; Announcement from the sponsor of our videos,&nbsp;&nbsp; who has chosen to remain anonymous&nbsp; to hide his incredible wealth. PSA: App Stores Have a Review Period! Hey guys, it's me, the sponsor of this video. Again, I’m definitely not Adrian.&nbsp; We look completely different. I actually work out. See this? Adrian doesn’t have this. Anyways, I have something important to tell you. App stores won’t let you just&nbsp; publish your app whenever you&nbsp;&nbsp; want - it needs to pass a review process. Their official statements say ‘just&nbsp; a few days’, but it can take longer. So whatever date you want to launch on, submit&nbsp; the app for review at least two weeks beforehand. Better safe than sorry. How do I know all this even&nbsp; though I’m just a video sponsor? Adrian taught me. He’s amazing. His&nbsp; agency is amazing. Subscribe. Subscribe. K bai. What do you mean I don’t have biceps? Anyways, two parts, remember?&nbsp; Algorithm first, users second. We’ll start with the algorithm.&nbsp; App Store Optimization&nbsp; Strategies for The Algorithm What you need to remember about&nbsp; algorithms is that their function&nbsp;&nbsp; is to promote relevant and quality apps to users. So your app store optimization strategy should&nbsp; convince them your app is worth promoting. We do this with Keywords&nbsp; Positive user feedback. Performance metrics Let’s start with keywords The specific term is metadata - just&nbsp; in case you ever come across that term. If a user searches for ‘apple’,&nbsp; the algorithm will look for apps&nbsp;&nbsp; that say ‘apple’ or something&nbsp; related like ‘fruit’ or ‘pie’. As far as we know, the Google Play&nbsp; Store algorithm looks for keywords from: The package ID The app title&nbsp; The short and long descriptions User reviews The Apple App store looks for keywords from: The app URL The app subtitle The keyword field The biggest difference here is that Google&nbsp; scans your descriptions for keywords. Apple doesn’t. Instead, they’ve got a keyword&nbsp; field that’s only visible to you. You provide Apple with the keywords&nbsp; you’d like your app to rank for. With Google, you just do your thing,&nbsp; and they’ll see if they like it. What can you do to maximize your&nbsp; keyword usage for either platform? Look up popular competitor apps and&nbsp; see what kind of keywords they’re using&nbsp; Don’t repeat keywords - use a&nbsp; variety provided they’re relevant&nbsp; Don’t keyword stuff your product&nbsp; descriptions - space it out&nbsp; You still need to appeal to&nbsp; users - remember to sound human&nbsp; If this is for something serious, consider&nbsp; paying for a keyword tool like App Radar. If you want to see good keyword usage in action,&nbsp;&nbsp; I recommend checking out Signal’s&nbsp; product pages on both app stores. They’ve used a variety of&nbsp; keywords across their sections,&nbsp;&nbsp; and it doesn’t read like a boring manual. Of course, Apple won’t show you the keyword field,&nbsp;&nbsp; but it’s a fair bet that a lot of Signal’s&nbsp; keywords will also be used in their description. Next up, we look at Reviews and ratings Positive reviews, negative reviews and no&nbsp; reviews all tell the algorithm something. Positive and negative reviews are&nbsp; pretty self-explanatory, I think. No reviews can be taken to&nbsp; mean your app is pretty meh,&nbsp;&nbsp; and a competitor with better&nbsp; engagement can overtake you in ranking. We’ll stick to Signal, let’s look at&nbsp; their main stats in the Play Store: Three-quarters of their two million&nbsp; ratings are five-star reviews. They’ve got a teeny tiny percentage&nbsp; of people who gave them 1 star. You can’t please everyone,&nbsp; and the algorithm knows this. If you have a million users and a&nbsp; hundred negative reviews, no big deal. If you have ten users and eleven&nbsp; negative reviews, very big deal. Your app is so bad that somehow even&nbsp; people who haven't used it don’t like it. But for MVP validation, you know&nbsp; that’s just what you need to do. To add on to this: Not all positive and negative reviews are equal Some users will say stuff like ‘attractive or&nbsp; unattractive design’ which could apply to any app. The best kind of review you can get&nbsp; are ones that validate your USP. In the case of Signal, they claim to offer secure&nbsp; messaging, so this is what they want to see: Those underlined parts are great because it&nbsp; contributes towards ASO from the algorithm. It’s not you saying you’re good at something,&nbsp; here’s a real-life user verifying it. This also means the opposite is true:&nbsp;&nbsp; a review that criticises your app’s ability&nbsp; to deliver what it promises is very damaging. As publishers and developers, focus on delivering&nbsp; great UX through an intuitive and attractive UI. Really understand your user&nbsp; journey, because then you can… Be strategic about when to ask for a review This is actually quite a bit less to do with&nbsp; marketing and more to do with product development. The two really do go hand in hand. If you want reviews, you need to ask for them. If you want positive reviews,&nbsp; choose when to ask wisely. You’ve experienced those annoying pop-ups&nbsp; on apps asking you to review and rate them. They’re like mosquitoes, and you&nbsp; know what we do to mosquitoes right? Wait until users have had a&nbsp; positive experience with your app. After they’re done making a purchase. Gotten off a call, Read three articles. Opened a mystery prize that&nbsp; gives them ten billion coins. Just don’t interrupt them while&nbsp; they’re getting shit done. Also, space out your requests. Don’t ask them every time,&nbsp; it gets irritating fast. If you’ve ever browsed Reddit’s website on mobile,&nbsp;&nbsp; you know they are relentless about&nbsp; asking you to download their app. I literally have to hit not now on every page. Thing is, I’m on Reddit a lot. I think I’d find the app useful, but I’m&nbsp; never downloading it just because I hate&nbsp;&nbsp; how Reddit put advertising&nbsp; above my user experience. And I’m not alone. Ok, now I’m done. Come back here. See, you just have to know when&nbsp; to catch people in a good mood. Of course, some people are&nbsp; never in a good mood, so be prepared to… Handle Negative Reviews Gracefully Your app could cure cancer. Someone will still find&nbsp; something to complain about. Don’t ignore these people. Reasonable or not, their emotions are valid. Also, your app is new, and every&nbsp; review carries more weightage. So respond to all of them,&nbsp; especially unhappy ones. Apologise for negative experiences. Mention that bugs are being patched. Address requests for features. If you want to be a successful founder, you&nbsp; need to be responsive and care about users. Make them feel heard and appreciated. Turn critics into your most vocal supporters. Converting a low rating into&nbsp; a higher one has been proven&nbsp;&nbsp; to boost your user conversion rate like crazy. Ok, that wraps up ratings and reviews. It’s more&nbsp;&nbsp; straightforward than keywords&nbsp; I think, just make sure you: ask users for a review at the right time, and address negative feedback That brings us to... Performance Metrics for App Store Optimization&nbsp; ASO for the algorithm doesn’t stop once a&nbsp; user downloads and starts using your app. It’s just begun. In fact, it never ends. The algorithm will now monitor&nbsp; how well your app performs. Things it measures include: Stability - how prone is your&nbsp; app to crashing, and where?&nbsp; Rendering time - how fast does&nbsp; it take to load the interface?&nbsp; Energy-drain - how power-hungry is&nbsp; your app compared to competitors?&nbsp; Startup time - how long does it take for&nbsp; your app to go from closed to running?&nbsp; Permissions - how keen are users on granting&nbsp; you access to their devices and data? For Google, this can be accessed via&nbsp; their Play Console, and for Apple,&nbsp;&nbsp; head to their App Analytics via App Connect. You need to check your stats regularly at first&nbsp;&nbsp; because there will always be bugs&nbsp; and you need to address them quickly. Past that point, always make sure your app’s&nbsp; performance performs at or above benchmarks,&nbsp;&nbsp; especially as the user base grows and your puny&nbsp; little no-code app gets stretched to its limit. Sorry, elitist developer side speaking there. Everything we’ve seen so far tells&nbsp; us how to speak to the algorithm. Hey, algorithm, I’m an app and I’m really&nbsp; good at providing a certain service. And the algorithm is like, prove it. So I go, Look at these keywords I’m linked to Look at my rave reviews&nbsp; Look at my zero crash rate I’m legit, bro. And the algorithm is like, ok, ok, pretty good,&nbsp; I’ll recommend you to my millions of customers. You’ve won over the machine, now for the humans.&nbsp; Strategies for User Conversion At this point, your app appears at the&nbsp; top when there's a relevant search. But it’s not the only option&nbsp; - there will be competition. You need to use every visual and&nbsp; text-based trick to convince the&nbsp;&nbsp; right kind of users to visit your&nbsp; app product page and download it. There’s slight differences between stores, but&nbsp; here are the ones you need to focus on for both. App Icon Screenshots,&nbsp; Video and Description Let’s start with App Icon Your icon alone should give an&nbsp; indication of what your app offers. Let’s look at Signal’s icon in isolation Judging from the speech bubble,&nbsp; we can guess its a messaging app Now let’s put it next to WhatsApp. They’ve both got speech bubbles, so&nbsp; they’re both definitely messengers. From there, the design differences are clues. Whatsapp has a phone - it’s telling users,&nbsp; hey, I can help you make phone calls. Signal has a line around it to&nbsp; represent a layer of protection - hey,&nbsp;&nbsp; I provide security for your messages. Look at the icon and the&nbsp; title as a whole, they fit. I have to say, I do think it&nbsp; could have been done better. I think on iconography alone,&nbsp;&nbsp; something like SafeSwiss does a better&nbsp; job of communicating ‘protection’. But hey, Signal’s icon is different enough to&nbsp; convince people, and that’s all it has to do. Let’s look at Signal’s&nbsp; screenshots from the Play Store. Here’s what’s good about them: They use Signal’s brand colour&nbsp; The screenshot is accompanied&nbsp; by clear and concise copy Signal’s five screenshots,&nbsp; plus the accompanying copy,&nbsp;&nbsp; clearly show and tell users what they&nbsp; can expect to get out of the app. Plus, it’s a chance to show&nbsp; their user interface in action. Signal only targets phone users, so&nbsp; they only need phone-sized screenshots. If your app is also optimized for tablets, include&nbsp; a couple of screenshots from a wider screen. Do I Need a Promo Video? Short answer: If you have to ask, probably not. Longer answer: Good quality video content&nbsp; is expensive and time-consuming to produce, Evaluate what a video can communicate to&nbsp; your users that screenshots alone cannot. Signal doesn’t have a video, they’re doing fine. Are screenshots not enough to&nbsp; clearly capture what your app offers? Does your app contain video&nbsp; elements that are crucial to the UX? If you have a case for a promo video, make sure&nbsp; you set aside enough resources to execute it well. And that’s it! We’ll recap everything now but&nbsp; first, holy crap that was a long one. When you’ve been doing it for so long you&nbsp; don’t notice how much actual work goes into it. Editor, can I have a cup of coffee, please? Thank you. ASO Strategies&nbsp;&nbsp; For Algorithms: Takeaways Level up your keyword game by&nbsp;&nbsp; doing research or paying for a tool Plan when to ask users for reviews&nbsp; Respond to negative reviews to&nbsp; convert them into supporters&nbsp; Monitor your app’s vital performance metrics ASO Strategies For Users: Takeaways&nbsp; ALL assets here must be pleasing to the&nbsp; eye and consistent with your branding&nbsp; indication of what your app offers&nbsp; Your screenshots should show&nbsp; users key value propositions&nbsp; include some copy to clarify what users are seeing Video is not always necessary Again, these are the basics of ASO.&nbsp; These are things you definitely need&nbsp;&nbsp; to know and need to do if you want&nbsp; your app to appear on anyone’s radar. ASO is a rabbit hole, you&nbsp; can go way deeper than this,&nbsp;&nbsp; into things like A/B testing and&nbsp; localized keyword optimization. Here’s my advice, all knowledge&nbsp; is useless until you need it. Right now you don’t need it. As a non-tech founder, you’re gonna&nbsp; be playing lots of different roles. You need to be a jack of all trades. Get your app optimized in the app or play&nbsp; store with these basic principles first. And because I know you’ve already forgotten,&nbsp;&nbsp; remember to submit your app for&nbsp; review with a 2-week buffer window! Whatever leftover time you have,&nbsp; pour it into product development. Thank me later. Or thank me now, by subscribing to&nbsp; my channel for more content on app&nbsp;&nbsp; development for non-tech founders. I’d like&nbsp; to cover more practical topics like these. Do&nbsp;&nbsp; you think the video was too long or was it an&nbsp; ok length? Would love to hear your thoughts. And If you want to see marketing and&nbsp; product development done simultaneously,&nbsp;&nbsp; check out my story on Alex Turnbull, a non-tech&nbsp; founder who created a highly successful software&nbsp;&nbsp; company with no technical co-founder. Alex made&nbsp; lots of great decisions and a few bad ones,&nbsp;&nbsp; I learned a lot from studying&nbsp; him and I think you will too.

The platform claims to offer expertbacked lessons, personalized growth plans, habit tracking, and structured routines focused on productivity, confidence, communication, and creator success. The app presents everything in short, easy to digest lessons, which is appealing for beginners who don't want long courses.
