- Why Indian D2C Brands Are Crashing in 2026 | Sahil Khanna | Case Study: Kind: captions Language: en A D2C brand crossed revenue of Rs 505 crore in 2024. The name is Sugar Cosmetic. In 2025, their revenue fell within just one year. There was a drop of approximately 17.8% above Rs 415 crore. Apart from this, its EBITDA reached above Rs 108 crore. - With a 26% margin. This story is not about empty sugar cosmetics. Wow skin science is done, mmer is done, good glam is done, wet is done. All of them are heading in this direction. Today I am going to tell you a specific number after which more than 80% of D2C startups fall. What are the three reasons behind it that even the VC will not tell you. I call this the 50 crore wall. Whatever I am going to tell you, I am going to tell you from my experience. I'm consulting a lot of D2C brands. So, I know a lot of data internally. Plus I will tell you the names of many companies publicly. I will tell you their data and show you exactly what is going on brother. First of all let me explain the context to you. As of 2018, India had 300 D2C brands. By 2025, this number has crossed 11,000. And the biggest thing is that out of these 11,000 brands, only 233 brands have crossed the revenue of Rs 150 crore. This means that only 2% of startups are able to reach this level. Along with this, another survey was conducted in which it was found that 60 to 70% of the D2C startups are stuck at the mark of Rs 1 crore to Rs 50 crore. They don't know how to proceed further. Look, I feel that the founders of D2C brands have become very smart these days. View content on the internet. Some also study. So they have learnt a playbook that we have to make a good product. After that it has to be marketed. Money has to be spent on meta ads. This has to be done on Google Ads. Influencer marketing has to be done on Instagram. After that, the ad of the same content has to be run. And somewhere it seems very easy to create a brand worth Rs 10, 12, 15 crores. And this is also the reality. If you keep your basics right, you can generate revenue of Rs 10-15 crore even without a very talented team. If the intention is right. The first level that gets hit is from Rs 20 crore to Rs 50 crore and after Rs 50 crore, it happens that the company may even close down. So if you are a D2C founder planning to start a D2C brand or investing in a D2C company, then today's playbook is going to give you a much better understanding. I still remember the time from 2018 to 21 where many startups were seen being created. Money was available easily. Growth was visible. This was a time which we can call the Golden Era of D2C brands. This was a time when CAG was less. Running Facebook ads was easy. Influencers didn't become that big. So you could get the video made from an influencer cheaply. Covid hit at this time. People were moving towards online. Organic reach used to be available on Instagram. And at the same time, Mer became a brand, Boat became a brand , Sugar Cosmetics became a brand. Many such popular brands have emerged. a customer could be easily acquired for between ₹300 to ₹600. Make a good product. Make a good story behind it. Sell that story on the internet, whether through ads or through influencer marketing. Just sell it. Bring traffic to your website or to aggregator platforms. There you scale yourself and your business is built. There used to be a simple matrix. Show the GMB to the investor, brother, our GMB is this much, it is growing at this much percent, GMB means revenue, we are earning this much money, now see, we have done this much in this many months, we will reach this much, the VC used to get impressed and give money, by 2023, the market seemed to be slowing down a bit, the growth that was expected was not visible, due to increasing competition, CAGs were increasing and many companies took smart decisions, like I will talk about mm meaning, many people criticized them at that time. But brother, they launched the IPO. They launched an IPO by 2023 with a revenue of Rs 1920 crore. Boat also planned to launch its IPO but could not do so. Tried multiple times but it didn't work. Shark Tank came to India. Many people became interested in startups. It gained a lot of popularity. The founders here were seen becoming influencers themselves. Taking the same founder as an influencer, ads were made, ads were run and better results were achieved somewhere. If I tell you from my experience, then 2022 was the peak for D to C brands. After that the world seemed to be changing. The first problem that arose was the high customer acquisition cost. How much money does it cost a person to sell goods? In 2021, this number used to be 650. Reached 890 in 2022. Reached 1180 in 2023. Within 24 days it reached 1520 and within 25 days it reached above 1850. Here I am talking about average things. We are spending ₹1850 to sell one item to one person. Which is seen increasing by almost 30% annually. Now let me give another data layer on top of this. If I talk about beauty in India, then the average order value here is ₹890. It is priced at ₹1250 in fashion. Meaning you are spending ₹2000, running ads and your sales are coming in at ₹800, ₹900. This means that for selling one product, you have to bear a loss of ₹800, ₹900 per customer. Now if I tell you the second big problem, it was the iOS 14.5 update. After which I would say the world changed a little. Where the accuracy of your targeting has been seen to decrease by 60 to 70%. We can track people. If you have visited my website. What did you see? What did n't you see? Based on that I can create a cohort of yours and show you ads. But after the iOS 14.5 update, the tracking accuracy which was 6070% seemed to stop. Many options have been created here that you can do this, do server side tracking, do this. But the reality is that the world of trekking is no longer what it used to be. Today, a pop-up appears in iOS asking whether you want to be tracked or not. Whoever it is, he will say, no brother, I don't want to be tracked. In fact, many such browsers have come into the market which people use so that they are not tracked. They have also created a system to prevent tracking within the safari. Google Chrome was also being taken in this direction. But at this time Google has stopped. So the simple thing that we used to know earlier was ROI. That ROI is not coming today. The third important thing here is creative faith. In fact, in the new update of Andromeda, creative has been made as a center piece, that at this time you do not have to do targeting because they cannot do targeting, so you do not have to target that much, but you should focus on the creatives, you should put messaging to the creatives as to how the creatives will be, the more the creative the better it is, but the story is this, how many creatives will you make as a D2C founder, you know that video runs and you have a cost for making video ads. You will have to spend a lot of money on every shoot. Yes, AI has arrived at this time. But many founders don't know that we can solve many of our creative problems by using AI. In fact, many DTC founders don't realize how important a role creative plays. The cost associated with every creative is going to be paid by growing their business. But they think why should a new shoot be done? Why spend more money on creatives? If AI can make you an intern for free, then that is better. Even if my brand gets diluted, it does not matter to me because we have to save the cause and somewhere it is not wrong. If I talk about the fourth reason, it is competition. There were 500 brands within 2020. By 2025, there are over 8,000 brands. All the ad networks work on budding. If I have to show an ad to a person at this time, then many people are competing to show that ad. The more people compete, the more the cost will appear to increase. According to research, CPM has increased by 40%. CPM means if you have to show an ad to 1000 people, then the average cost has increased by 40%. And we are sitting in that era where people are watching the relay with full force. Time consumption on social media seems to be reaching an all-time high. But brands have grown so rapidly that your costs appear to be increasing just as rapidly. Now let us assume that a founder puts his heart and soul into it and says no, I will take myself to Rs 50 crore. He is working properly till Rs 20 crore. It is operating automatically. There is a lean team. They are operating from the same warehouse. Limited are their SQs. Someone tells them that brother, if you want to grow your revenue then you will have to increase the number of orders. To grow revenue, they will have to expand their warehouse. You will have to increase the size of your team. This will have to be done. That has to be done and let's do it. After doing this, they reach Rs 50 crore but the condition of the business becomes very bad. There is tension. Losses appear to be increasing. Now let me tell you a breakage which is reality. If a startup is generating a revenue of Rs 50 crore, then how much money does it actually bring home? Let's start by talking about a real case scenario. Let's assume that the cost of the good is around 35%. If you have sold goods worth ₹100, then you actually have to pay ₹35 for the product. Whether you manufacture it yourself or buy it from somewhere, you are left with a gross margin of 65%, which is considered good in the industry. 40% of your money is spent on marketing, which again is considered good, that you have spent around 40% on marketing, in which you have done meta ads, influencer marketing. You may have multiple agencies. I have paid them the fees. Money has been spent on creative production and so on. Your logistics cost comes around 15%. In which your RTO is also included. I will tell you further that RTO is a big problem. But I assume you have optimized it by 15%. Your RTO comes at 15%. Team expenses are around 12%. So if the entire amount is calculated then there is a loss of Rs 1 crore. We have faced a lot of tensions behind doing a business worth Rs 50 crore. We have incurred a loss of Rs 1 crore. And right now it is above the contribution level. Here, if you have to pay any interest, there is depreciation, taxes have to be paid, there is any consulting etc., I have not included any of that. So, in short, a D2C brand incurs a loss of around Rs 34 crore to generate a revenue of Rs 50 crore. And the biggest thing is that when any brand does Rs 50 crore, is making losses , onboards investors, then a decision is taken, it is celebrated. Let's scale. Scale here means that we will spend more money , bring in more SQs , and expand offline. You will be seen expanding into new categories. Take the example of Mm. When they launched their IPO in 2024, their revenue was Rs 1920 crore. Where he spent Rs 664 crore on advertising. Meaning they spent around 34%. Talking about Quarter 4 of Financial Year 25, this net profit was seen declining by 17%. Their revenue increased but profit appeared to be decreasing. Take the same story of Sugar Cosmetics. Its revenue in the financial year 24 was Rs 505 Within the financial year 25, it came down to above Rs 415 crore. Meaning a decline of almost 18%. Talking about Bau Science, it had a revenue of Rs 340 crore in the financial year 23. It reached Rs 233 crore within 25 days. Where they reduced their revenue by 30% to reduce their losses. If all three brands are considered together, the story is the same. An attempt was being made to expand offline. Due to which their margins appeared to be decreasing. If you sell offline, you have to spend around 40% over and above the MRP. Which goes to your distributor, goes to retailers, trade pay promotions, returns have happened. In all these things, where online you sit on 65% gross margin. Offline pay reduces to just 40-45%. The second big thing was they expanded their SKUs very quickly. There was a brand that came from face wash. After that he started selling shampoo , body wash, body lotion and skin care. Started selling lipstick and eyeliner also. Meaning, we will sell everything that we understand. But let me tell you one thing which is a reality, the more you add SQUIDs, the more your cost gets included in it. Behind every new SQ is a new inventory cost. There is a risk of returns. There is a fear of losing customers. And the third and biggest thing is that these people were running after premiumization. The customer who is buying your goods for ₹300-400, you are telling him that brother, now buy the goods for ₹900. It's a simple matter, the audience buying a product for ₹400 is different from the audience buying a product for ₹900. But you are thinking that this product should be sold to the same audience. All these things that I am talking about, we can see them in numbers. 3900 startups are expected to shut down by 2024. Around 2200 startups are expected to shut down by 2023. Yes, not all of them were D2C brands. But majority of the startups were D to C brands. Take the biggest example of Glam Group, brother. At one time this company had reached a valuation of above Rs 2000 crore. He acquired Sirona for Rs 450 crore. Scoop for under 100 crores. Acquired Miss Malini. And by 2025, the condition of the company became such that Bhai Sirona had to sell it back for Rs 150 crore. Scoop, which was bought for Rs 100 crore, had to be sold back for Rs 20 crore. Their strategy of creating a House of Brands remained only on paper. In reality nothing came out. I have made a case study on this. I will give it to you in the i button. You will understand if you see it. What mistakes did he make from which we can learn a lot. Look, running a D2C startup is a death spiral where you burn money in the startup to grow your revenue. When the money runs out, you go to the investor. The investor says burn more money. You burn more money. After that, when the money is over, you go to the investor again. The investor says, brother, you are burning too much money. Show me the profit. Reduce your expenses. Now the company reduces its expenses in order to reduce costs. Due to which the customer leaves them. The company 's revenue appears to be declining. After that another funding round is taken. Efforts are made to revive the company. Many companies revive themselves. Many companies are unable to extinguish it. Now the simple thing is that whatever funding you get is in a way increasing your runway. If the foundation of your business is bad then the business will sink, if not today then tomorrow. If your foundation is bad because you scaled too quickly and the investor came. The investor is giving you money so that you can fix your foundation. There is one year's time, try it and see. If everything goes well then we will launch an IPO. You will earn money with pleasure. So this means that brother, D2C brand should never be run. D2C brands will never be big. This won't happen. Look, let me tell you some realities. First of all, every product has a cake ceiling. When you start selling your product in the market, you target early adopters. You make offers. Those who use it for testing, within no time a revenue of Rs 10-20 crores is generated. At that time your CAKE remains between 300 to 600. As soon as you move towards the race of Rs 20 to 50 crore, your cake shoots up there. Because right now you're not looking at the early adopters or you're not looking at the customers who are just experimenting. Now you are trying to cater to the people who are also catered to by your competitors. You are raising funding for the product you are selling, it is not just you, there are other people in the market who also want growth, so because of this our CAG increases and reaches ₹10 and as soon as you cross the ceiling of 50 crores, CAG is seen touching the number of ₹1500 or ₹500 because at this time you are competing with very big legacy brands, be it Hindustan Iver, Marco, PG, you are going into that category, if I am selling a soap today, then I will sell the soap to a small audience but going above a level will be a big waste because there are many people who have been using Dettol for centuries. A lot of people are using Dove. A lot of people are using pierce. That's the brand they have in mind. She is running the family. They have been using these brands since childhood. Nastya has been created. He does not want to do new experiments. This product is running in the family. They are sticking to this. So now you have to burn a lot of money to break those people and bring them to your brand. Let me tell you another important thing. That is the LTV trap. Most of the founders think that the business will be built on LTV and that at present there is no problem if we incur loss. We will earn money in the future because the customer has taken a call on us today. We have also given him a discount of 30%. After that the customer will like our product. So brother, that will be repeated. No no brother, you have just given a discount of 30% to the customer. That's why the customer has come to you. Will the customer return at full price or not? This is a very big question. Companies say that brother, we have customer retention of 30%, 40%, 50%. Meaning customers are taking us. But here there is a big flaw in his calculations. The first thing you do is customer repetition in which window cycle? First of all tell me whether the customer bought it today and then after a year, is he a repeat customer or not, did the customer buy it on his own, that means he came to the website, opened my product and bought it or you have given a discount to the customer by running an ad and after that he bought it or you have formed a cohort, brother, these people buy it, you are running remarketing ads to them, you are paying them again and then you are bringing it, if the repetition cycle of any product, let us say, is of 15 days. The customer should repeat after 15 days. You can take it for 1 month or one and a half month. I just think the first one is a huge flaw within the repetition rate window. Most of the founders just touch it and say, brother, this is our repetition rate. They manipulate the data itself. Yes, okay. You have to tell a story to the investor. The world has to tell a story. If I say that brother, my repetition rate is 50%. 60 means people will say wow, what a great product it is. People are taking it again and again. But that repeated definition is not clear. Apart from this, there is another big thing which most people are not talking about. That is RTO. Brother, up to 25%, 30%, 35% of RTOs go to D2C in India. Do you understand the meaning of 30% RTO? 30% of the goods were sold and the return came. The cake that was on it was gone. That is zero. If you had spent ₹1500 to acquire a customer, it has become zero. Many people do not even subtract this RTO from their actual numbers. They see that my ROS is coming at 2.50. But have you subtracted your RTO from that ROS ? The second big problem is do you know what is inside it? CAG is gone, okay. Your product may also be lost. Many times that goods gets lost on the way. Many times when the goods are returned, they are in such a condition that you cannot sell them again. You have to do its packaging again. In 80-90% of the cases, the packaging itself is damaged. Suppose you have to pay for reverse shipping. Meaning you sent that goods to him. Then when I returned from there, I had to pay twice for logistics. And the biggest thing is that whatever delivery system is there, it is taking 10, 12, 15 days. Where your 10, 12, 15 days of inventory, capital, your cash flows are just stuck in the air. And on top of that, theft is going on inside the market. Where delivery attempts are not being made. The goods have been sent to the customer by the founder. There it was shown that brother, the customer was not at home. In reality, no attempt was made to send it to the customer's home. But he said that brother, he was not a customer. That's why that product was returned. There is no fault of yours here. But you also ruined your CAG because he wanted to take the potential product. Here you have also given two time logistics costs. Plus the capital block is different. Now the question arises here that is there no possibility that any brand cannot become big in D2C. Let me give you some examples where you will get some clarity as to what can be done if you want to become big in D to C ? First of all let's talk about Mokubara. People might be hearing the name Mokobara a little more now because Farah Khan is gifting it to a lot of people. But brother Mokobara is building a great business. There was a revenue of Rs 53 crore in financial year 23. It reached Rs 119 crore within 24 hours. It went to Rs 240 crore in financial year 25. Their revenue doubled. The company has become EBITDA positive within the year 2024. And do you know what is the biggest reason behind this? The average order value itself is very high. They are working towards the premium category. Where goods worth more than ₹10,000 are being sold. These people are selling luggage, selling travel accessories. Due to which their average order value is so high. If the average order value is good and you have an omni-channel approach because you are making a good margin on each sale, then your business will be seen growing. Today it is present in Bengaluru, Delhi, Mumbai and in fact even in Dubai. I think they have caught their niche and the combination of pricing they have done and have realized very early that brother, our customer will buy our product after feeling it, so we will have to crack offline. That is the biggest reason behind their success. Second one of my favorite brands minilist. I have also made a case study on this. Take a look at that. You will understand in detail. What magic did Minimum Millis do brother, Hindustan Unilver bought the company for 2000 crores, their projected revenue is 500 crores, where I am saying that it is difficult to break 50 crores, they are generating 500 crores, where Mama is sitting on 200 sq, these are working on 30 sq, where Mama Earth has done influencer marketing with full force, you cannot see their direct influencer marketing, to know in detail see that case study, I have explained it in full detail, the third recently emerging brand is Snatch. Brother, what a story it is. If seen, it is in the men's wear category. Almost Rs 500 crore revenue is being reported by 2025. We have doubled our revenue from last year. It is profitable. Working on omni channel. The biggest thing is that we are operating on fashion. Meaning new clothes arrive within three weeks. The best thing is their content team. You are generating very good content for your audience. According to me, he understood very early that only Bhai Conant would make a brand big. How long will you keep burning money on Facebook and elsewhere? If you want to sell any goods then there are only three important things for it. Someone please trust me. The founder of the company should come, the team members of the company should come, the spokesperson should come, trust him, love my product and love my company. They have thoroughly captured their audience and based on that they are catering to all three things through social media which I think is a very smart strategy. Everyone should learn. If you look at everyone, the biggest reason for everyone's expansion is offline. So D2C brands have to go offline. But you also have to consider that if you go offline, your margins take a hit. So can we think from the very beginning that we have to become a brand worth Rs 500 crore? If we want to become a brand worth Rs 500 crore, then we will have to go offline and involve ourselves in reverse calculations and not in the starting calculation that brother, my product is worth this much. This happens, this happens. No, this is my revenue. My CAG will come this much. This much will come to me. This much will come. So my product cost should be this much. If you go over reverse unit economics , it becomes easier to build a business. Like I told you, COGS should be around 35%. This is the industry standard. But brother, if it is not working in my calculation then I can take 35% to 30% or 20% also. In fact, in many categories it goes up to 10%. If I feel my marketing game is much better. I can do a better game by mixing social media with influencer marketing. The industry standard will be 40%. Can I bring my marketing cost down to 30% or 25%? And I am telling this clearly. This isn’t going to happen with a blank Facebook ad. A consistent long-term social media strategy will be followed where we create content to engage our audience and not just create it. For example, if I talk about logistics and warehouse, then at this time I am telling this from my experience. If you do fast delivery within 2 hours, 3 hours, 4 hours then your RTO reduces. At this time, there are many such startups which are working in major cities where you can store your goods. Your order will arrive within two to three hours and your goods will be delivered. At present, there are no complaints here that delivery was attempted but it did not happen. The market standard will be 15%. If I work properly, I can bring this 15% down. I can reduce my RTOs. If I do fast delivery then. See what are the reasons of RTO ? First, let's assume the customer placed the order by mistake. Ordered by mistake. So a very easy way to fix this is that whenever we send the goods, we should get it cross checked beforehand. Whenever we send the goods, before that we should drop a message on WhatsApp through automation that brother, your goods have been delivered. Your goods have been packed. This has happened to you, that has happened to you. So from this we can know whether the customer is interested or not. There you can give the option of a single button that if you want to cancel the order, you can do it. So if the goods have not yet left your house and someone cancels the order, then there is no tension. We saved money on logistics. The second reason could be that the person who placed the order wanted the product but actually the goods were not delivered to him. Meaning there was no delivery attempt. The lie that is going on in the market did happen. So if you work with honest delivery people then this problem seems to be solved to some extent. The third important thing is that as time passes, I am able to check more alternatives. Like I just bought a product. After that, after taking the goods, this is everyone's habit. Be honest in the comment section. I search for reviews. Brother, I have already purchased the product. After buying I am watching reviews on YouTube. I am looking at reviews everywhere. I should have looked before buying it. But I'm still watching it even after taking it. I am trying alternatives. So, if my delivery time is delayed then the customer's mindset may change due to that. He may have different options. So my order may get cancelled. Which I think is a huge portion of cancellation. And the last one which again has a big portion is fraud orders where competitors kill the orders. Where people forcefully order touching. So its solution is being made but it has not been made yet. You can say that brother, install AI layer , get the address checked, backlist the pin codes. A lot of them have just become checkout systems. Adopt them. But this problem has not been solved at that level yet. This will happen as our Indian e-commerce will be seen growing. And the biggest advantage of delivering within two to three hours is that your working capital remains in your hands. Meaning, the goods come and go immediately, the goods come and go quickly. If you see that more goods are sold in Delhi then get a setup made in Delhi. Get a set built inside Mumbai. Get a set built inside the South. Make a setup in those states from where you are getting maximum orders. Keep the goods there. Now let me tell you one special reality brother. 2018 to 23 was a golden era where more than Rs 15,000 crore was raised from VC. Of which around 60% was also burnt. From which no output was seen coming out. Now were the founders wrong here ? According to me he was smart brother. He raised good money during good times. Was hard working. Was skilled. Was the VC wrong? Not according to me brother. He was also running his business. They can see it. There is growth. If I invest money at this time, that money will appear to be increasing. My business will be built. We will launch IPO tomorrow. I will get a very good exit. According to me, what has happened here in the Indian ecosystem is that the quality of the product has become commoditized. Meaning you are buying things just by looking at the price. You are buying things after seeing the discount. Let's take an example. Korean beauty is very much in trend right now. Where beans are working inside the bean packaging. All of them are running the same meta ads. Everyone's business is growing to a certain level. But actually the business is not getting established. Many people think that growth is business. But growth is not a business, brother. It is a simple thing here. When I am about to hit this 50 crore wall. Do I have enough funds to cover my expenses for 3 years now? Mama Earth is the biggest example of mmer, when its IPO came, it got a lot of criticism that they don't know how to do business, this is not the case, that is not the case, but after the money they raised, the business seems to be growing, right? Yes, okay, right now the profit seems to be decreasing, but the business seems to be growing, talking about wow, they go and expand globally, they went to the US, but they came back from the US because they have money to run themselves, talk about Sugar Cosmetics, suddenly after Shark Tank, brother, they took the money and after that, they started expanding aggressively offline, right now they are settling themselves a bit. This can only be done by those who have capital. It is a very bad thing to dream big without capital. And this is not your fault. According to me, a structural problem has been created which I don't know how it will be solved. Most people think that they should raise money. Funding is a solution. But funding is actually not the solution. Whenever you are going to take funding, you have to keep three things in mind. What is the first path to profitability going to be? How will I become profitable? By what date will I be ready ? By which quarter will I be ready? Are my specific numbers behind that? Am I meeting unit economics targets ? Is it going to be cake? How will my cake appear to grow over the period of time ? What is my real LTV? I would like to say real LTV because I explained to you that LTV is whatever is going on in the market at this time. Those who are saying that 50% is our repetition rate, their actual repetition rate is 10%-12%. And the most important thing is brother, what is my burn rate? How much money am I willing to spend per month? If it goes above this, brother, we will stop. If you plan to raise money with this discipline, then race. Otherwise you are heading towards a ditch. There will be a wall, you can try to jump over it but you won't be able to. If you fall again and again, you will break your bones. I think there will be a new wave again in 2026 where AI tools have arrived. Creating ads has become easier using AI. Using AI, you can create websites very quickly using agents. Meta Ads Google Ads is moving towards a bit of automation. Where you do not have to hire big agencies to run basic ads. Yes, this is not like 2019 where you will get money easily. Just make a good product , raise money, burn money and build a business. But somewhere you can control your CAG through effective use of AI. Can't reduce it. Brother, CAG will keep increasing over the period of time. Unless all the companies come together and decide on one thing that brother, we will not do this. We will not burn that much money. So it was December 2022 when it was released and we decided to leverage this opportunity to build an AI first product which was Flame AI. >> So Chad GPT came out you said like late 2022 and then you immediately hopped on the kind of like GPT rapper wave and then how did that go? At first >> retention went up from 0.8 to 4.2. >> Okay. So that's day you said that's that was day 30 >> retention day 30 and u we had to do another pivot man just to be very honest with you this flame this AI rapper was enough to get us in the media get us published like you see we had another article in techrunch February 14th Valentine's Day we came out as the AI product for relationships we also featured on LA Times or a lot of other places but we realized that it wasn't working like even though we could get media presence even though the retention went up to from 0.8 8 to 4.2 it was still dismal and we realized we had to do something else. What we did is this product had AI as its main feature but along with that we also launched something called the daily question where we would give couples a daily prompt they could answer together. Now I know it's really common right now and almost every other app does it but back in that time we were one of the few like I think paired was the only one apart from us which was doing it. We realized that people were coming back not for the AI coach but for that daily question feature. They were coming for a daily way to connect with their partner. And we took that nugget we extracted it from the product and we made it the main stay in the product. We put it right on the homepage and big we were like hey daily discovery question this is the main thing we want you to come back for. Now what I did regarding retention is I studied the work of a Stanford professor called BJ Fog. He works a lot in habit formation and we realized that every retention event needed to be tied with a trigger. It needed to have something which was a daily habit to call people back on a regular basis and it needed to have a carrot and a stick approach. What was the carrot and the sick approach in this case? In the morning if you would answer or not answer your daily question when it was live, you would get a carrot notification reminding you, hey, come back, answer your question, take care of it. And as the day passed, if you did not answer your daily question, we would send a stick notification reminding you that, hey, it's time for you to answer the daily question. You're going to lose your street. Your partner is going to be sad. Your partner is going to be missing out. Um, and it worked really well for us. And we were able to increase our day 30 retention from 4.2% to roughly 30% and then all the way up to 50% by doing this. 50%. That is a pretty big number. But can you put that into context for some other consumer apps? That means basically 50% of the users will still be there on the 30th day that they open the app from the time that they downloaded the app. So is that good or bad? Like how how good is that? Because spoiler alert, it's actually an insane number. But I want to Can you give some context to it? >> Yeah. So we studied a couple of other big social apps to understand what her retention was looking like. If I remember correctly, X has a 30-day retention of 30%. And uh so we knew we were doing something right when we touched those numbers. It took us a lot of pivoting, lot of anchoring the product, a lot of working on the main feature to unlock this. And if I'm not wrong, we invested roughly around 8 months to get to this point. So it took us eight months and continuous iteration on the product. I did one big mistake though, Joseph, in this which you'll agree with. I focus on the product too much. I didn't focus on the distribution enough. So what happened is we were in the end of 2023. Now that is a big advantage we got. You losing the streak was not linked to you losing the streak. It was linked to you making your partner sad. >> Mhm. >> Do you see the difference? you doing something to get a carrot was not you getting a carrot, it was your partner being happy. And I think this is where we got a big advantage because this was an emotional product. This was linked with not only what you did, but also what your partner did. Right? You'll hear of these incidences where people go above and beyond to make sure their partner is happy. This was kind of similar. They didn't want to make the partner sad. They wanted to make the partner happy. So they would make sure they would come back on the product, answer the question so the partner would remain happy and they wouldn't end up missing it in the end of the day because they didn't want to make their partner sad. >> Yeah, that makes a lot of sense. I paid for it and I thought I would be clever. I would use the same Apple ID to log into every phone. So I could download the client for free. I won't have to pay for it again and again. It cost us a week of our work time. That is 40 hours for three people, 120 hours. Why? Because we realized once we did that that Tik Tok has a way of fingerprinting Apple IDs. And when you do that across multiple phones, it connects all these Apple IDs and the accounts link with them under one device. So it doesn't matter if you had a different IP, doesn't matter if you had a different setup on a different phone because we use the same Apple ID, all of these accounts were connected and one account getting flagged led to the other accounts being impacted as well. >> Interesting. So yeah, while we're putting out content on Tik Tok, we realized a lot around how do we exactly put out content, how does it get enough amount of views. Now, one thing we learned while putting out content on X or Tik Tok is that if you have under 300 views, then there's an issue with your account. Either the content is getting shadow flagged or your account is banned and your account isn't private to that happening. If it is under 300 views, then it's not a content problem you have, then it's an account problem you have. Then you have done something wrong with the setup. You're not pushing it out in the right manner. You need to ideate on it further. You need to move on it further. If you're getting over 300 views but under thousand views then it's something to do with your content. Then you need to iterate more on your content. Then you need to hone down deeper into it. Then you need to figure out try different formats, try different hooks, try different things which are working in the content. If you get between 1 to 5,000 views, then what we do is we continue with that same format, same type while changing the copy slightly until it hits the right marker until we end up crossing 100k views with that format type. >> So is that basically how you so you have a video here that's 340,000 views. And that kind of gets you into the just into the flow state of knowing more of the art of it and like figuring out why did this actually do well or why didn't this do well. I think tools are really good when you're doing UGC because you can't go into huge piece of content, analyze the retention chart, see where people are dropping off, how many, what total watch time did this video get. But when you're putting out your own content, the best way, I think that's the golden gem you have, right? That you can actually dig into the content, analyze it, see what is happening, say where people are falling off. And you stop doing that if you're using a tool. You stop doing that if you're using something that's automatically tracking the views. You don't need to go into each account. You don't need to track what's happening. You don't need to see why things are working. You pick the top accounts and you see why things are working, right? But as you realize, Joseph, the the cream lies in realizing why something is not working rather than in realizing why is it working because when something doesn't work, you get to learn so much about what are you doing wrong and not repeat the same failures again. >> Yeah, that makes sense. And yeah, all of these all of these insights are going right back into the master prompt and the AI is now like generating better and better outputs. Um, it looks like you have like a nano banana image prompts too, which is cool. >> Um, that's like, yeah, one of the newer models. And you're doing like hyperrealistic vertical images, and that's probably good for like slideshows. >> Exactly. And we tried playing with a lot of models. We realized Nano Banana Pro was the best at generating realistic lifelike images for us. And we also realized that we could standardize the whole process to get the output from GPT regarding what kind of image should we generate and the prompt to generate that image as well. >> Is there anything you do in prompting that you feel is different from other people that you've seen? >> Two things I would say. One is the initial prompt we set in place. We try to give it as much context as we can. I know people who go to it and say that you know I want to put out a slideshow for Tik Tok. Help me generate something. That is really shallow. I think AI is this tool which works really well. The more you give it, the better context it has, the better it knows what is winning, the better it's able to give you something that wins as well. This is the first thing we do. Well, we provide a lot of context initially like to create each of this playbook. It takes me one working day. It takes me a whole day to put up this entire playbook. I have to collect the right formats. I have to collect the right screenshots. I have to make sure that they align with whatever product is. I have to make sure that I create the master form to firstly give an output that has the potential to go viral but also give an output for what we need which means it should have the title slide it should have these categories it should have an anobanana prompt as well once I do all of this then I put that into AI and then I test the prompt to see what is the output coming out if it comes out well then I pass it on to the team if it doesn't come out well then I go all over the process again this is one of the key steps which is setting it right in the first go the second thing which a lot of people ignore is iterating on it right because this is like us as well we have new information that comes in every single day we have new data points we have new way to improve on what we are doing but for your AI playbook is the same it needs to improve as well it needs to become better and it can only do it if you tell it what to do better if you expect it to get better by itself it doesn't happen as I told you as a second part of the process as well we feed it data back on what's working well we keep iterating on this playbook again and again and telling Hey, this works well. This doesn't work well. You know, it's like a cursor MD file for coding, but this is for Tik Tok. It's like a Tik Tok MD file you create. >> Yeah, that's crazy. One like random specific question. When you're iterating on the master prompt and you want to put in some new insight, do you use GPT or Claude to help create a new adjusted prompt or do you manually put in new context into the prompt? I do it manually because one thing I realized is that once we have the initial prompt structure laid out, it's more about me coming in and filling the gaps. I've tried doing that with AI but there are times when AI hallucinates or it fills some things which I don't want it to fill and I have to go over recursive or process of making it correct that path again in this manner. I know that we have something that's working really well. We keep that and I just keep adding on top of it or removing the parts that are not working. >> Mhm. So it's like it's like you know AI bakes this entire cake for us and then I come in and put the cherry in the right place or make sure the decoration is aligned well or the happy birthday sign is in the right manner. >> Mhm. Yeah. Okay. That's interesting. When you reach out to creators and you're about to invest into organic social and maybe boosting those videos with paid ads, you don't want to just let the creators do whatever they want. Most of the app founders I talked to on this channel basically have the same content philosophy. The videos need to go viral but also convert and promote an app and ideally are like also repeatable. And those formats are like gems and really hard to find. That's why Tik Tok can sometimes feel like a lottery system. Like I had to pull out some of the content on it because we were just we just terrible at it. >> we made this account back two years ago and we had no idea what we were putting out, man. We managed to scale them to 50 million views with one caveat. It took me in the later part of those views to realize that certain kind of content performed better, certain kind of content did not for conversions. So I look at it from the BSC framework whenever we are picking out something. Wii is virality. I'll actually share this with you as well. So I put out something on X regarding how do we exactly find content. >> So we follow the VSC framework in which Vi is for virality. Something that can go viral. How we find this out? By picking content that has gone viral on accounts with less than 5,000 followers. Like we have a very structured approach for this. And if there's a piece of content that has gone viral on accounts with less than 5,000 followers and the content has got over 100,000 views, then we understand this content can go viral. Then what we try to see is is it trending right now? Is it present across other accounts as well? If it is, then it bears this check. Second part is scalability. Now this is something a lot of people ignore, but it's really important when you're doing Tik Tok farming at scale. If you cannot scale the content, if you can just have one hit, then it doesn't work for your product. You need to have content you can recreate yourself at scale and do it consistently to push it across five 10 accounts at the same time, multiple times a day. If you can't do that, man, if you have this brilliant video of you speaking and elaborating on something, but you have to manually record it every single time, it doesn't work. So find something that you can scale. Right now, what I would recommend everyone to check is we have a lot of AI tools available which allow you to do that. We use Sora quite extensively. We used the influencer.ai. We also use nano banana as I showed you. We use nano banana pro. We tried putting out videos with VO but they always got flagged for AI content. So we always found struggle with VO when we putting out content. And then the final part which took me too late to discover but which is the most important is the C part which is the convertability. Now this is the most important. If you go to the comments, if people are not asking what is the app, what is the product, where can I get this etc. then it is not content that converts. Then it's content that's getting views. Then it's content that's going viral, but it's not content that can convert. You need something that converts. You need something that's going to force people to try to find the product. They want to use the product. They're desperate for the product, right? They really want the product to be there. And this is the VSC framework we now utilize in pushing out anything. For me, with the 50 million views we came across, we struggled with the C part. Cart is something we didn't focus at at all. I'll tell you one of the things, right? the meme video you see, I would never put that out right now. Never. Even though it got views, it crossed over 300,000 views, I would never put that video out right now because I realized that that format doesn't convert. It's a lot of the work you're putting in from our side for a format that is not converting. >> Yeah, that that's a really interesting insight. And so the point is you really have to be one of those top desile companies if you want to build a venture scale company. Um, one of the things that I talk about in some of my materials that I'm not going to spend a ton of time talking about today, but kind of the highlight here is subscription apps are easy to launch. They have all sorts of built-in advantages, right? They don't require complex network effects. You get all sorts of turnkey support tools within the app stores from Apple and Google. So, they're very easy to get off the ground. And that's now true more than ever with AI, which we'll spend a little bit more time talking about, but they're very difficult to scale because they have high churn rates, much higher churn than B2B SAS businesses. They don't have strong network effects in many cases. So you don't get the lock the lock in effect uh that you have with two-sided marketplace places and they're increasingly reliant on major ad networks like Meta and Google for acquiring new users and those networks are getting more and more crowded as the app stores get more crowded. So this sort of paints the picture of why building consumer subscription businesses is so challenging. So now what's the solution? Like what do we do about this? If you're an early stage startup or you're a bootstrapped soloreneur trying to build your company, what you want to do is look to some of the top companies, the the apps like Duolingo, Straa, Quizlet, the category leaders, and look at what they're doing to support long-term sustainable growth. And so this framework that I've developed is called the subscription value loop. And basically what I posit is that the center of every successful subscription business is a unique and enduring core value promise. And what I mean by that is it's unique in that it stands out from all of the other competitors in your category. And almost every every category in the app stores is is crowded at this point. And then it's enduring, meaning it's providing long-term value to subscribers, which is why they continue to pay you month after month and year after year. And then outside of that core value promise, you have these three key steps. Value creation. This is led by core products product teams. It's the specific products and features that your company is building to support your core value promise. Then value delivery. Value delivery is generally led by marketing or growth product teams to distribute your product into the hands of your users. Uh and then value capture, which is making sure you're getting enough revenue back from your users in the form of subscriptions or sometimes ads if you have an ad supported premium model um to reinvest uh dollars as as resources back into product development and into paid marketing campaigns. So as your value promise gets stronger and as you get better and better at these three steps, value creation, value deliver and value capture, two key things happen. One, your LTV over CAC ratio, which is your customer lifetime value over acquisition cost, that should go up. And you want that to be over 3x ideally for for venture capitalists to be excited about your business. And then your payback period comes down. And the best consumer subscription apps are paying back their paid CAC within the first month or or even on the first day of acquisition. their their ROI positive on their acquisition costs almost immediately, which just means they can reinvest that cash back into growing the business. Okay, so there's the theoretical underpinning of our talk for today, which is the subscription value loop. Um, but how do we put it into practice? So unlike most products that start as point solutions, Notion launched as this full suite. It's basically the wiki for your company where you've got databases and you've got templates and you can use it for all these different tools, right? And so what they needed to do was rely on their early adopters to really explain and evangelize their product. And um and then they also created digital artifacts in the form of templates that could be easily shared on social media channels like YouTube or Tik Tok. And so when notion launched notion AI in 2023, they were one of the first companies to really go all in on AI. They went back to the well with the same strategy and they really leveraged this evangelist community that they cultivated over many years to help get their new AI products out to market and explain to users how they should be getting value out of those AI products. Um, number three is using AI tools to build and test hundreds of creatives per month. So, I know a lot of you are probably relying on meta ads or other paid acquisition channels to grow your businesses. AI tools like 11 louns for voice, sunno for music, or bo3 for video have become really powerful ways of just generating more content more quickly to power your paid acquisition budgets. And then this last example also features a creative team that's done very very well by by using AI tools similar to RA but also by highlighting their AI features. So Photo Room is one of the early movers in the photo editing and creative space and they had this background removal and background editing feature for a long time. But when AI came out in 2022 and 2023, they were able to significantly improve that feature. And so for a while, Photo Room was primarily generating revenue from users in the US and in Europe. And they had never really been able to crack markets like Mexico, Brazil, Indonesia because their unit economics just wouldn't support it. It cost them too much to acquire a user in those markets relative to the amount of revenue they were getting back in terms of LTV. Well, what happened was when they were able to launch this new AI powered background feature um and they really emphasized it in their um paid acquisition creatives uh and specifically optimized the first six seconds of their video ads to feature this this um new launch. It caused not only casts to drop significantly, but it also caused trial starts and subscriber conversion rates to go up, which increased LTVs. Um, so all of that meant that their unit economics improved radically and it actually unlocked the ability for them to move into new international markets like the ones that I mentioned before. Okay, so that covers um our four tips for value delivery. Um, and just to recap what those were, uh, we have number one, leveraging PLG loops to amplify word of mouth SEO and AEO. Number two, investing in community to drive organic growth through UGC. Number three, using AI tools to build and test hundreds of creatives per month. And then number four, we just talked about highlighting AI features to reduce CAC and unlock new markets. Um, Yan has a question here. What have you seen to be the main differentiator beyond innovation for apps like Quizlet and Study Fetch? Yeah, so obviously innovation is huge and and it's the cornerstone of value creation, right? If you're not building products and features that are new and different in any way, then you're not really creating incremental value. So that is the engine that is the that is where the engine for the subscription value loop starts. I would say one of the other things that that's become increasingly important though is distribution. So value value delivery and on my pod I'll share a link to my podcast at the end of this. Um there are a number of episodes I can point you to but um I I recently uh had an episode where I talked a lot about why um AI is making distribution more than important than ever because it has reduced the cost for software development. It has democratized software to the point where now you don't have to be a technical founder or even have a technical co-founder to be able to build a software product because you can buy code an app in a weekend and get it to market very quickly. So what that means is the bottleneck has moved from being able to build a great app to being able to distribute it efficiently. And that's why if you look at Quizlet for example, one of the keys to Quizlet success was it's just very very good at acquiring users for free through organic channels like SEO and word of mouth, which has meant it's been able to grow for years and years and years with much less outside capital than a lot of other companies because of these organic growth loops. Um similarly, I talked about Gamma. you know, Gamma just raised their series B, but they had actually spent almost none of the money that they had raised in their series A from Excel by the time they raised their series B. Why? Because they were growing through these organic loops, so they didn't have to spend money on paid acquisition. Okay, so let's get to our third and final step, and that is value capture. And I would say this is probably the most overlooked step in the loop. It's pretty obvious that you need to build a great product and you need to find costefficient ways to deliver it. I would say where a lot of earlystage companies fall down is they're overly generous in giving away way too much value for free, which means they're not recapturing enough value in the form of subscription revenue to reinvest in product innovation and paid acquisition. So it's really really important to strike the right balance there. Otherwise, you can get yourself in a pretty difficult place. So three tips here. Number one, offer two subscriptions with basic versus AI powered features. This is really really important because it used to be that the marginal cost of serving a subscriber for most traditional subscription apps like Duolingo or Straa was close to zero, right? Like it's just server costs. There's very little else that goes into serving the marginal subscriber. Yeah, you've got your fixed costs like your your employee costs and your healthcare benefits and office space and equipment, but in terms of marginal costs, it's next to nothing to serve the the incremental subscriber. Well, that's no longer really the case for AI companies because they're using compute costs with these underlying LLMs to support AI powered features. And so what that means is companies are having to really change how they approach monetization. And one of the specific ways they're doing that is they're they're offering two different subscription tiers now. One with basic features that don't require AI at a much lower cost and then one with AI powered features that's much more expensive because it has to offset those underlying LLM costs. And a great example of that is Dolingo. They were again one of the early partners with chatbt when they released their API in and they used it to build a set of AI powered features like roleplay and um video call with Lily that made their way into Dualingo Max which is their new AI powered immersive language tier. Okay. So that's number one. Offer two subscriptions with basic. >> Yeah. It's so obvious but uh like almost nobody uh it >> Yeah. I mean, and for a long time, like enterprise SAS businesses typically have multiple subscription tiers, >> but for the most part, for years and years, best practice was if you're a consumer app, only offer a single subscription tier because consumers don't have long attention spans and it's just too complicated. But I think that conventional wisdom is changing now because it's just not tenable to have a single subscription tier. If you if you only have a single subscription tier, then you're going to be overd delivering value for a lot of users who can't afford to pay for the AI powered tier and just want your basic features. But then if you give your subscription price away for too low to serve those users, then you're going to lose money on your whales who are using your AI powered features all the time. >> Yeah. >> All right. Tip number two is similar, which is limit trial links and premium offerings to minimize AI cost. So in the same way that you want two subscription tiers, a basic and an AI powered tier in order to cover your LLM costs, you also don't want to give away too much for free in terms of seven or 30-day free trials or premium offerings that are just too generous. And so more and more apps are moving from 7-day free trials to three-day free trials, which is the minimum that that uh the Apple App Store allows. Um they're also more tightly restricting their free product experience. So if you have a premium offering, they're not giving away as much for free. They're putting usage caps on AI features in some cases even for premium subscribers and then they're selling additional AI credits as inapp purchases. Um, and then that relates to the third and final tip here, which is charge subscribers for AI credits beyond a certain usage cap. I think for a long time it's been taken for granted that okay, if you pay for a subscription, it's just all you can eat. Once you pay for the subscription, you get everything. Whether that's tools in the case of a product like Dualingo or Straa or it's content in the case of a product like Netflix or Spotify or calm. Um but that now that's not always the case anymore. And so the example here is 11 reader. This is the consumer app built by 11 Labs. I actually just had Jack McDermott, who's the head of growth uh for mobile at 11 Labs on my podcast, and he talked about all the different payw walls and pricing and packaging techniques they've iterated through at 11 Labs for both their 11 Labs app for creators and their 11 reader app for consumers because they needed to make sure that they were offsetting these underlying AI costs. They have users who use their products 10 plus hours a day. And so even if they're paying for a subscription, in some cases, they're still unprofitable. They're underwater. if you're not setting a cap on usage or adding AI credits beyond that subscription cost. And so they've really innovated on their monetization strategy to make sure they're aligning the price of their product with the value and the cost that users are extracting from the business. Okay, so those are all of my tips across value creation, value delivery, and value capture. The three steps of the subscription value loop, which is my framework for growing subscription businesses. Uh we can go to uh Q&A section if we have any more questions. I saw a few more come through. Um, let's see. So, can selling additional AI credits to paid users backfire as a strategy? Uh, certainly it can. It's a great question, Jane. To me, the tight line you're trying to walk here is obviously the more value you give away for free, the better you're going to do in terms of net promoter score, product market fit score, long-term subscriber retention, word of mouth, almost being equal, like you're just building goodwill with your subscribers when you do that. But the flip side is if you have a large percentage of your subscriber base that is actually unprofitable because the amount of cost they're generating in terms of compute exceeds the amount they're paying you in their monthly or annual subscription, then you don't have a business. You don't have a viable business. And so at some point that's not sustainable. I would say the degree to which you want to go all in on just giving more away value away for free versus making sure you're charging for that value whether whether that's AI credits on top of subscription or otherwise comes down to a few factors. One is how big is the market you're going after and is it a blue ocean market or is it a highly competitive market. So an extreme case would be you know chat GPT versus claude or openAI versus anthropic right like they are the two largest labs models going up against Google and Gemini in this massive blue ocean market that is horizontal LLM lab and so in a lot of cases even though their products are expensive they're in in in some cases they're losing money on some of their most valuable users and subscribers or at least I'm that would be my conjecture um but it makes sense for them as a long run strategy because they're in a dog fight for market share and they don't care so much about squeezing out every last penny of profitability from the business in the short term. What they care about is being the number one player in the market because ultimately that's going to lead to a much larger enterprise value over the long run. So that's on one extreme end of the spectrum. But on the other extreme end of the spectrum, if you're a bootstrap startup or you're a solarreneur, you're a small seed or series A funded company, you only have so much capital. And if you're racking up these huge LLM costs, then you're going to run out of money. And so I think you just have to keep that in mind. Um, so that's that's one factor. Another factor is to what extent are free users or subscribers generating value for you that goes beyond the financial value you're getting from the revenue they're paying you. And so we talked about Quizlet before. In a case like Quizlet, even non-subscribers generate a ton of value for the business if they're highly engaged because they're talking to if they're a teacher, they might be sharing it with their class classroom, their students. If they're a student, they might be sharing it with their classmates or their friends or their siblings. Um, so that's word of mouth. They're also creating content in the form of digital flashcards called sets, which then get crawled and indexed by Google and drive a huge percentage of Quizlet's growth through organic user acquisition. And so another factor you have to consider is you know are these users generating non-financial value for your business in the form of organic user acquisition or other factors. So it's not a simple solution like you need to consider all of these different variables when thinking about how generous or not generous to be about giving away AI credits versus charging for them. But I would say up until maybe a year or two ago, the idea of paying charging your subscribers for additional usage beyond what they already did in their subscription would have been absurd. Like nobody would have really thought to do that. And that has now changed. More and more of the top AI companies are doing this. Um or they're just capping you. Like I'm sure many of you have used cloud and cloud code before. I think we've all had the experience where you're in claude code, you're doing some important project and then it's like you run out of credit. Sorry. you have to wait for five hours before you can start using the product again. So that's another option if you don't want to charge people. Okay, Pablo asks about basic and AI featured offers. Where do you think is the right moment in the user journey to show both tiers? Yeah, that's a great question. So um there are a couple of experts out there who talk a lot about payw walls and specifically payw wall placements. And this is oversimplifying it, but at a high level, the common areas to trigger a payw wall are before onboarding, immediately after onboarding, on subsequent app launches, whether that's every app open or, you know, one out of five, one out of 10 app opens or certain days of the week or so on and so forth. And then the last one is contextual upsells where you're actually triggering a payw wall based off of the usage of a specific premium feature. So at the very highest level I would say think about those four different placement opportunities for your pay walls before onboarding after onboarding. So see an app opens and then triggered off of current feature usage. And then as far as like which tier you expose to the user it depends on which placement we're talking about, right? So, like if it's pre-onboarding, you probably want to show both tiers because at that point, you don't know anything about the user or which tier they're more likely to be interested in. If it's at the end of onboarding, well, maybe you only show one tier or the other because depending on their answers to onboarding questions, you may already know this person is more likely to be a basic subscriber versus an AI subscriber. Or you show the tier that you think they want as primary, and then there's this tactic where you can put more plans at the bottom. And that way if you guess wrong, they could still click more plans and then you could show a side-by-side comparison. If it's on subsequent app opens, I would say the advantage there is, okay, now you not only have their onboarding quiz responses, but you also have all of their activity up until that point, which you can use to customize. Do we show them the AI tier or the basic tier? And then if it's a contextual upsell, then the answer is obvious, right? Because depending on which premium feature they engage with, if they're engaging with a premium feature that's only available in the AI tier, then obviously you're going to show them the AI tier. If they're engaging with a feature that's available in the basic tier, then maybe you show the basic tier, but you also say, "Hey, do you want even more features or more functionality? Um, what are the biggest levers to increase conversion in the onboarding quiz? What would you recommend to test first? Yeah, so onboarding, I mentioned this at the beginning. So we've worked with 35 clients since inception at Elemental Growth. Um, one of the first things we work on with almost every client is onboarding. And the reason for that is there's just so much leverage tied up into user onboarding because it not only increases activation rates, it also increases engagement rates, trial start and trial conversion rates, and long-term subscriber retention rates. It's all sorts of research that shows that the new user experience isn't just important for those first few day metrics. It's important for long-term retention for the entire user journey because you're setting the tone. It's your first impression with with a user. And so as far as what that means for the onboarding quiz, I would say there's a few things like one, making sure that you're asking the right questions to better understand your users and segment them into different groups. Number two, using that to personalize the experience. And the optimal personalization is going to be what I talked about with RUA where it's like literally the core product experience is adapting around that user's responses. But if you're not at the point yet where your company can support something that advanced, even things like what I call credibility building screens or social proof screens where you're inserting a couple lines that explain, hey, this product is built for users just like you who XYZ based off of their responses to quiz questions and then having a carousel of like three to five star app ratings or five star app reviews that are specifically dynamically generated to look like the type of user that just went through the onboarding quiz. is those are a couple tactics that any company, even if you're a soloreneur, you could be using and and those tend to be very effective because it means that your new users are feeling heard. They feel like, okay, this product understands me. It's not just treating me like a number. It understands my unique needs, and then it's showing me other people who've used the product successfully, gotten value out of it, who look like me, and it's telling me what I need to hear to believe that this product is going to generate value for me. So asking the right questions, personalizing what users see based off of responses to those questions. I would say onboarding length is a big one. I had a LinkedIn post about a year ago where I showed the difference between the All Trails onboarding flow and the new onboarding flow, which are at two in extreme ends of the spectrum. That's for good reason, right? All Trails already has a web product. So if all I want is to like find a trail, I can just look that up on Google and find an All Trails landing page and I don't need the app. which means if I'm downloading the app, it's probably because I'm already on the trail ready to record a hike. And so in All Trails's case, they keep the onboarding flow super short. It's basically registration page, upsell, payw wall, and then let's get started. In N's case, it's the opposite because they're trying to help people lose weight. In many cases, they're dealing with people who've tried all these other weight loss programs, weight watchers or whatever the case may be. they haven't worked. And so they need this long onboarding quiz or what's often referred to as a longboarding quiz in order to build motivation and ask questions and personalize the weight loss plan to really convince this user that this is going to work where all of these other attempts they've they've tried to lose weight have failed. And so it's two opposite extremes in terms of how long the onboarding quiz should be. And that is very much a function of who their target customer is and the problem they're trying to help that customer solve. >> Yeah. And on boarding on boarding quiz also could be uh could provide the value itself. Uh so for example this example with the weight loss if you show like um prediction how uh when they lose a lose the weight and uh hit the target target weight it's uh could be value for for users as well. So it's like it's answering this question and understanding this themselves why they want to lose weight, why they want to do that and uh and when they can do that it's uh it's already value itself. >> Yep. Absolutely. Yeah, that's right. Like in the best cases, you can actually generate value right away in the onboarding quiz. You don't even have to wait for them to get into the core product. You're generating value simply by asking them valuable questions, teaching them about weight loss and and certain strategies that will work and then convincing them that your personalized solution for them is going to work or others whether other solutions have failed. And then the last one is pay walls, right? Like having different messaging on the payw wall depending on how they respond to onboarding quiz questions. Um even potentially offering different prices. So having dynamic discounts based off of how they answer certain questions or the type of user they are. All of those are valuable strategies. I guess the last one and um I have a great podcast episode with Greg Stewart who's the CEO of Ladder, which is one of the top fitness apps in the country right now, specifically on strength training. He talks about how they predict the LTV of every user based off their responses to quiz questions. They've gotten it all the way down to the level where every incremental response to a quiz question gives you an incremental LTV value that they can use to sum up the total predicted LTV of a new user. and they pass that information back to ad networks like Tik Tok and Instagram and then they can use that to go after more highv value users based on those predicted LTV values. - Publish an app
- Run Shopify App Ads with a wide set of keywords
- Let the campaign run for a week
- Use impressions and visibility to see which keywords actually have volume

Alternatively, can you use an existing app that’s not closely related, run a small ad budget, and still get meaningful keyword impression data?
Or does Shopify heavily limit impressions if the app category and functionality don’t match the keywords?
For example, would it make sense (or be totally pointless) to use a simple free app like a popup banner app to test keywords such as checkout customization?

- Offer the right subscription - App growth: Kind: captions Language: en APP GROWTH OFFER THE RIGHT SUBSCRIPTION Are you matching the value that your application offers to users with the right subscriptions? If you aren't, you could be losing out on a valuable regular revenue stream. In this video, we look at best practices for successfully implementing subscriptions with the right paywall, length, and price. Let's start by looking at the paywall options. There are three you can choose between: Gated, Metered, All-or-nothing. Option one: Gated paywalls. Often seen as the "freemium" experience. Gated paywalls are used by apps to offer exclusive features, content, and functionality on top of a core offering that is open and accessible to all users. This approach suits apps that bring users together around social experiences and user-generated content or support goal-oriented activities. This paywall type focuses on engaging users further in their lifecycle supporting organic growth and long-tail monetization and it's often combined with in-app purchases or ads monetization. Option two: Metered paywalls. Metered paywalls are quota-based and allow users to experience the full functionality of your app or view content on it for free, but only up to a certain quantity. This paywall type brings you extra value in being experiment-friendly and transparent to your users. Option three: All-or-nothing paywalls. Also known as a hard paywall this approach puts all your functionality and value behind a subscription paywall. It often relies on brand awareness and a clear value proposition to convert subscribers. This approach suits apps that bring users a steady stream of original or licensed content. Once you have the right paywall for the type of content you offer your next challenge is setting the right durations for your subscriptions. Here you can apply some general principles. Shorter-duration subscriptions, those of a month or less are best suited to content or features that: change regularly; are often seasonal. Longer-duration subscriptions, those of three months or more are good where: your users are committing to a long-term goal; you want to offer a discount to users for their loyalty. Now you need to consider your subscription pricing. There are four best practices that have been shown to help price subscriptions that appeal. One: Diversify. Offer users subscriptions at different price points. Develop tiers and discounts to maximize conversion. Two: Price anchoring. Promote the highest lifetime value subscriptions to your users and use free trials to incentivize your target SKU. Three: Communicate savings clearly. Make discounts and special offers easy to understand for your users. Four: Localized pricing. Ensure that you're setting prices based on country benchmarks, genre trends, and exchange rates that are local to your user. So, for a successful subscriptions offering choose the paywall that fits with your app's value proposition and set subscription durations and prices to appeal to your users. Dive deeper into the Google Play resources on unlocking your app's potential. Check out the links in the video description. Stay tuned for our next video, and let's grow together.
