Is he BS-ing you? Partially — here's the honest breakdown:
Where he's credible: Dan Martell is a legit entrepreneur and his tier rankings are mostly reasonable. The advice to avoid trading bots and faceless YouTube is genuinely correct, and he's honest about it. AI consulting, agent development, and voice agents are real opportunities with real paying clients right now.
Where he's selling you something: The whole video is a funnel. He's promoting his "Sell by Chat" playbook (DM him), his Apex platform (AI agent tool), and his personal brand. Every idea he rates S tier happens to require skills he teaches or tools he sells. That doesn't make the advice wrong, but you should notice the angle.
The 5-step launch process he describes at the end (validate → pre-sell → launch manually → build audience → then productize) is actually solid and battle-tested. That part is worth keeping.
Most viable picks for a normal person:

AI consulting — if you have domain knowledge in any industry, this is the easiest entry. No code needed.
AI voice agents — the tech is accessible (Bland.ai, Vapi), local businesses genuinely don't have this yet, and the sales pitch writes itself.
AI lead generation — fastest path to getting paid, because you're literally delivering revenue to clients.

I'd also love Apple to allow home key customization. But permissions still feel way too blunt because in a real household, not everybody should have the same control over certain devices. For example, children should absolutely be able to control lights, play music, and adjust temperatures in their rooms, for instance, but they probably shouldn't be able to access opening garage doors, unlock exterior gates, disable security systems, or some of those devices that are a lot more sensitive. We need proper smart home roles, admin roles, standard users, and child accounts along with temporary access for guests because this even becomes more important if Apple launches a wall-mounted smartome display. Most people don't want to sit there building if statements and conditions like their programming software. Not the average user anyway. The system itself should start recognizing patterns. So, for example, if you manually increase the temperatures every day in the evening, Siri should notice that. It should notice that and suggest automating it. If the temperatures start dropping at the same time every night, it also should recognize and suggest proactively to adjust the temperatures. That's where AI becomes actually useful in the home, not smart lights that use magical AI things and all those gimmicks. This is where AI really makes a difference. Because CarPlay already knows a huge amount of contextual information about you, your heading home, your current route, your estimated arrival time, traffic conditions, and your usual routines and driving patterns. But Apple needs to massively expand this idea because your smartome shouldn't just react when you arrive. It should prepare for your arrival intelligently. So imagine this. You're driving home from work and about 45 minutes away. And through CarPlay, it could simply ask, "Would you like to start preparing your home?" And this is where intelligence really matters because it shouldn't just instantly turn everything on at once. Using proximity and ETA data, Apple Home could gradually warm the house as you get closer. Turn lights on when there is arrival actually expected and prepare the rooms you normally use first and adjust lighting also depending on the time of day. And if the house has been empty all day, it could even follow that up by proactively suggesting things like, "Do you want me to run your robot vacuum before you get home?" That's the difference between automation and intelligence. Because right now, most smart homes operate on fixed triggers and timers. But the future should be contextual. Your home should understand where you are, what you're doing, when you're likely to arrive, and what normally happens through your routines. And I know, I know earnings call doesn't exactly scream exciting Apple news. It sounds like something you'd fall asleep to halfway through, but buried in that call was a very important hint about the iPad 12. Parrick mentioned that Apple expects its iPad revenue in the current quarter to face what he called a difficult compare compared to last year. And while that sounds like corporate jargon because it kind of is, it tells us something very simple. Last year, Apple launched the iPad 11 with the A16 during the same periods. So naturally, sales are boosted. This year they're basically saying, don't expect the same boost. And the only reason for that, there's no new base iPad launching this quarter. So, just like that, we can confidently rule out the iPads while releasing before the end of June, which means WWDC is off the table. No surprise iPad reveal. No John Turners pulling an iPad out of nowhere for the crowd to go wild. It's just not happening. Now, at first, that sounds like bad news because for a while, it felt like the iPad 12 was right around the corner. We had leaks, we had code references, we had reports saying it was basically ready to go. And to be fair, that part hasn't changed. According to Mark German, the iPad 12 is essentially finished, like sitting somewhere in a factory, fully boxed up, just waiting for someone to give the green light, which makes our situation even more confusing. If it's ready, why isn't it here? And while this is where things get interesting because it starts to look less like a delay caused by problems and more like a deliberate timing decision, Apple's very particular about when it releases products, they don't just throw everything out at once unless they absolutely have to. And right now their focus seems to be elsewhere. We've had the MacBook Neo getting a lot of attention recently and if Apple dropped the iPad 12 at the same time, it would immediately split that attention. People would start comparing the two and suddenly neither product gets spotlight Apple wants. So instead, it looks like the iPad 12 has been politely asked to wait its turn. And honestly, that makes sense. Apple loves giving each product its own moment. Also, do remember that Apple's actually been struggling to meet demand for the MacBook Neo because they've run out of bin A18 Pro chips. So, amongst all this supply chain mess, releasing another in- demand product like the iPad 12 might not be the best move. Now, the question is, how much longer are we talking? Well, based on everything we're hearing right now, a launch later this year seems pretty likely. If not summer, then probably September. And yes, I know waiting until September for a base iPad sounds a bit excessive, but it wouldn't be the first time Apple's done something like this. We have seen base iPads launch during September before. Also, there is a small possibility, which I know some of you guys won't like, that it slips even further, maybe early next year. I don't think that's the most likely outcome, but the fact that rumors have gone a bit quiet recently doesn't exactly scream imminent launch. Now, let's talk about what we're actually getting when this thing finally does show up. because while the timing is uncertain, the specs are relatively consistent. The biggest upgrade here is going to be the chipset. Right now, the current base iPad runs on the A16, which is fine, but it has one pretty big limitation. It doesn't support Apple intelligence, and that's becoming a problem because every other iPad in Apple's range already supports it. The iPad Mini, the iPad Air, the iPad Pro, they're all part of Apple's AI push. Meanwhile, the base iPad's just sitting there like it missed the group chat. So, the iPad fixes that. We're expecting either the A18 or possibly the A19, depending on which report you believe. And honestly, either one gets the job done, both support Apple intelligence, which is the main goal here. Now, whether you actually care about Apple intelligence right now, is a completely different question because let's be honest, in its current state, it's not great. It exists. You can use it. You might even enjoy using it for 5 minutes before going back to doing whatever you were doing before. But the important part is where it's heading. Apple is clearly building towards a much smarter version of Siri, much more useful AI tools, and deeper integration across the system. And when that happens, you don't want to be stuck on that one device that can't keep up. So, even if it doesn't feel central today, it probably will in a year or two. Alongside the new chipset, we're also expecting 8 gigs of RAM. And this is actually a bigger deal than it sounds. More RAM means better multitasking, smoother performance, and apps staying open for longer in the background. It also ties directly into Apple intelligence since those features rely quite heavily on memory. So again, even if you're not planning to use any of these AI features straight away, this upgrade still makes a pretty big difference in everyday usage. Beyond that though, don't expect anything dramatic. The design is expected to stay pretty much the same, which isn't surprising because Apple already refreshed the base iPad design in 2022. And since then, the design has basically become the standard across the entire iPad range. So, we're likely getting the same flat edges, same display, same overall look. Maybe a couple of new colors if we're lucky. Although, knowing Apple, they'll probably recycle the same exact colors again. I'm still holding hope for something a little more interesting, though. Give us an orange iPad or a proper green. The base iPad is the one place Apple can have a bit of fun with the colors, so they might as well lean into it. Now, of course, because this is still the cheapest iPad, some compromises are going to stick around. The display will almost certainly remain non laminated, and yes, I can already see the comments. Why hasn't Apple fix this yet? Is it really that hard? Short answer, yes and no. They could change it, but it would increase the cost. And this iPad exists to hit a very specific price point. Also, and I know this isn't the most exciting explanation, but this iPad is often used by students and kids, and those two groups aren't exactly known for treating devices like fragile museum pieces. So, having something slightly easier to repair isn't the worst thing. This also means no OLED and no 120 Hz refresh rates, but the iPad Air doesn't even have those yet, so the base iPad definitely isn't getting them anytime soon. And honestly, that's fine for this price. Because at the end of the day, this iPad's all about value. It's meant to be simple, reliable, and affordable. And as long as the performance is solid, which it will be, most people won't care about the rest. Now, for those who aren't patient and can't be waiting weeks or months for the new iPads, I suggest two things. You can either get the iPad 11 today at a discount, which is still a great iPad. I have one. And well, the iPad 12 won't get it as I explained earlier, but the iPad Air already has this and is available for slightly more than the iPad 12. It might get less support because it's an older iPad. Just make sure you and any friends who want to use it update to the latest software first. Let's take a look at how to use Freeform on your iPad. To start, open the Freeform app. On the left sidebar, you'll find options for viewing all your projects, recent canvasses you've worked on, shared boards, and more. When you want to start a new board, tap the Compose button at the top right of your screen. We're going to check out a board we've been working on with friends. for personal projects like mapping out a road trip or planning a volunteer project. But the single stock options premium, I think there's a really nice opportunity to uh potentially sell some upside calls and call spreads, which is what we do. Le >> let's talk about a couple specific strategies you lay out. One product you highlight looks like dividend paying companies. Why why that? So let's say you want to open a bakery chain. So what do you want? First we need wheat. Flour is made from wheat. Dough is made from flour. Dough means kneaded flour. And then a loaf is made from that dough. The loaf is sliced and the slices are used to make sandwiches. And the sandwich packs into a lunch box. That is, wheat, flour, loaf, slices, sandwiches and lunch boxes. Simple. Now remember this because solar panel is made using the same process. Instead of wheat, silicon is a natural element which is found in sand, mountains and rocks. It is very common in the world. Instead of flour, polysilicon is purified at extreme temperatures through a highly controlled process and this creates polysilicon. This is the actual starting point of solar manufacturing. Instead of flour, ignite polysilicon is melted and cast into a solid block. Like putting a loaf of bread. This is called ingot. Wafers instead of slices. The ingots are cut very thin or even thinner than a hair by machine. This is the wafer. Solar panels instead of sandwiches. So chemicals and layers are added to the wafer, treated, and it becomes a unit that can generate electricity from sunlight. This is the complete process of solar panel. And what happened to the lunch box? Solar panel. Many solar cells are joined in a frame and a solar panel is made, lunch box from wheat, solar panel from sand. Now understand the process because this is where the real game starts. Where are the highest margins in a bakery? In bread or in flour? Imagine if you sell wheat, you are just a commodity seller. The price keeps fluctuating. You have no control. But if you make the flour then one step value is added. If you make loaf then more value is added. If you make a sandwich then more value is added. And if you directly pack lunch boxes and supply them to school canteens, then you are on the full value chain. Correct? Same thing happens in solar. Silicon, which comes from sand, is an almost free commodity. The real game is in polysilicon and on the wafer. And herein lies a huge problem that investors ignore. China controls 80 to 90% of poly silicon and wafer production. What does this mean for investors? This means that if you are buying shares of an Indian solar company that only assembles panels, then who controls its margins? In the hands of China. That means only he can decide. So when China makes polysilicon cheaper, the Indian assemblers benefit. When China tightens, its margins decrease. This is the same problem as the bakery. If you are making sandwiches and buying the dough from outside, there is a price taker. You are not a price maker. So will India's solar sector remain just an assembler? No. There is a shift here too. India's Yellow Scheme Production Linked Initiative is specifically targeting this problem. Why? Because the government wants India to do integrated manufacturing. Meaning, from wafer to panel, some companies in India are moving in this direction. Wafer capacity is being set up. Planning for the policy plant is underway. So now what is the signal for the investor that whenever you see a company in the solar sector, the first question you should ask is where is this company in the value chain or does it just assemble the panels ? Margins will remain thin because it is dependent on China. Wafer or cellar manufacturing is slightly better. There is a little more pricing power. If it is integrated, that is, from polysilicon to panels, then these are the companies on which it is wise to bet for the long term. So if everything is fine now, this is the same logic as the bakery that grows its own wheat. She grinds her own flour , makes her own bread, runs her own shop, her margins are her strongest point. Because they have their own margin at every step. And where do we see the same story ? Have to look into solar. ELLEN: I AM TALKING ABOUT THE PAPER MARKET SHOULD BE HIGHER, GIVEN THE INCREDIBLE RATE AT WHICH WE HAVE SEEN INCREDIBLE, BIG DRAWS FROM U.S. STOCKS, AND OTHER COUNTRIES ARE ALSO DEPLETING THEIR STOCKS OF OIL. BUT THIS IS A SITUATION IN WHICH WE ARE DEPLETING WHAT WE HAVE STORED, SO PRETTY SOON, THINGS SHOULD BECOME MORE EXPENSIVE. YET WHAT WE ARE SEEING IN THE PAPER MARKETS IS THAT THE PRICE IS NOT REFLECTING THIS. THAT'S HAVING SOME REALLY INTENSE REVERBERATIONS AROUND THE ECONOMY, BUT ALSO IN THE OIL INDUSTRY IN THE UNITED STATES. WE ARE ONLY HEARING RIGHT NOW FROM SOME PRODUCERS IN THE UNITED STATES THAT THEY PLAN TO EVEN INCREASE PRODUCTION, WHEREAS THIS CONFLICT HAS BEEN GOING ON FOR OVER TWO MONTHS NOW , AND THE PRICE INDICATOR SHOULD HAVE ALREADY BEEN THERE. TO BUILD ON THAT, BECAUSE AN INTERESTING DYNAMIC IS STARTING TO EMERGE. WE ARE SEEING PHYSICAL PRICES COME DOWN BECAUSE OF THE INVENTORY DRAWDOWN. I WONDER IF YOU EXPECT THE PAPER MARKET WILL TRADE ABOVE THE PHYSICAL IN ANTICIPATION? THAT IS ABSOLUTELY A POSSIBILITY, BUT THE PAPER MARKET IS SO DETERMINED BY A HEADLINE OR A TWEET OR WHAT PEOPLE IMAGINE IS GOING TO HAPPEN WHEN THE REALITY IS NOT JUST WHAT IS HAPPENING PHYSICALLY WITH OIL BUT ALSO WHAT IS HAPPENING THE ACTUAL STATE OF AFFAIRS BETWEEN IRAN AND THE UNITED STATES. THEY ARE MAKING IT LOOK AS THOUGH AN END TO THIS CONFLICT IS JUST ONE 14 POINT PAPER AWAY WHEN THAT IS NOT TRUE AT ALL. EVEN IF THERE IS AN AGREEMENT ON SOMETHING -- WHICH, BY THE WAY, THERE HAS NOT BEEN AN AGREEMENT ON ANYTHING OTHER THAN THE CEASE-FIRE. SO WHAT WE ARE OPERATING ON, OR WHAT PEOPLE ARE TRADING ON IN THE MARKETS, IS NOT NECESSARILY WHAT IS HAPPENING ON THE GROUND IN THE MILITARY SITUATION, NOR IS IT WHAT IS HAPPENING ON THE GROUND IN THE PHYSICAL SITUATION WITH CRUDE OIL. BUT LOOKING AT DUBAI CRUDE NOW, IT IS UNDER 00 A BARREL. ISN'T THAT ASTONISHING GIVEN EVERYTHING THAT YOU HAVE LAID OUT? ELLEN: IT IS. BUT ALSO, WHO IS GOING TO BUY DUBAI CRUDE? HOW MUCH OF IT IS ACTUALLY AVAILABLE? HOW CAN YOU ACTUALLY GET IT? CAN YOU ACTUALLY GET IT? IS THERE ANY GUARANTEE YOU CAN GET IT IN TWO MONTHS OR THREE MONTHS? THERE IS DEFINITELY A MAJOR DISCONNECT GOING ON HERE. IT IS HURTING THE ABILITY OF PRODUCERS THAT CAN PRODUCE MORE TO JUSTIFY PUTTING THAT PRODUCTION INTO PLAY. I THINK THAT, IF WE WERE RECEIVING INFORMATION ABOUT A SITUATION WHICH IS MORE ACCURATE, THAT COMPANIES AND OIL COMPANIES AND TRADERS WOULD BE ABLE TO TAKE POSITIONS THAT WOULD MORE ACCURATELY REFLECT THAT. AND I THINK WE WOULD ALL POTENTIALLY BE BETTER OFF. HOW ACUTE IS THE SPIKE HIGHER GOING TO BE, IF AND WHEN WE ARE STILL IN THE SITUATION AND THE GLOBAL SUPPLIES AND INVENTORIES ARE COMPLETELY DRAWN DOWN?
